blurb: Crater Mountain (8,132 ft) is a rugged summit in the Hozameen Range of the North Cascades, within the Pasayten Wilderness roughly 5 miles east of Ross Lake, on the Whatcom/Okanogan county line. It is built of metavolcanic greenstone and metasedimentary rock of the Jurassic-to-Permian Hozameen Group; its glacially carved, crater-like summit basin (not of volcanic origin, despite the name) holds the small Jerry Glacier on its north side. A fire lookout occupied the summit from 1932 to 1968 (established as a camp in 1929), staffed in 1952 by poet Gary Snyder, whose season there later informed his writing.

routes.routeName: Standard Route (Southeast Ridge via Jackita Ridge / Crater Lake Trail)
routes.overview: A long approach-heavy trail hike from the Canyon Creek Trailhead up Jackita Ridge Trail to Crater Lake basin, then an off-trail southeast-ridge scramble past a false summit to a loose, exposed 3rd-class gully near 7,600 ft that is marked with fading yellow paint and cairns, finishing on scree/talus to the summit plateau and old lookout site.
routes.beta: Widely regarded as a fun, non-technical but sustained 3rd-class scramble; the crux is a short, loose, exposed gully section below the true summit that is marked with painted yellow X's/arrows and requires careful route-finding among several cliff bands. Most parties do it as one very long day (~15 miles RT) or split it with a camp at Crater Lake.
routes.approach: From the Canyon Creek Trailhead (1,900 ft) on SR 20 (32.8 mi east of Marblemount / 55.1 mi west of Winthrop), take Jackita Ridge Trail #738, fording Canyon Creek and Granite Creek near the start. The trail climbs steeply through timber via numerous switchbacks (roughly 55) gaining about 3,400 ft over 4 miles to a junction near 5,000-5,260 ft, with views opening onto Crater and Jack Mountains above timberline. Turn left/west onto the Crater Lake Trail (brushy, user-maintained) for about a mile to Crater Lake basin (~5,000-5,700 ft) below the peak's south side.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route: downclimb the marked gully back to the southeast ridge, drop back into Crater Lake basin, and retrace Jackita Ridge Trail to the Canyon Creek Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, descend the scree/talus slopes back to the top of the crux gully and downclimb it carefully, following the same painted yellow X's/arrows and cairns used on the ascent (trip reports note this section is loose and exposure-sensitive both ways). Continue around the false summit's west side back onto the southeast ridge, drop into Crater Lake basin, and rejoin Jackita Ridge Trail for the descent to the Canyon Creek Trailhead, re-fording Granite and Canyon Creeks.
routes.itinerary: Day trip: pre-dawn start from Canyon Creek TH, ~4-5 hrs up Jackita Ridge Trail to Crater Lake basin, scramble the SE ridge and crux gully to the summit, then reverse the entire route — commonly completed as one very long single day. Alternatively, backpack in and camp at Crater Lake (permit required) to split the ~15-mile round trip and ~6,300 ft of gain over two days.
routes.bail: The route has no true technical bail points below the crux gully — retreat is by reversing the ascent line. Above the gully, downclimbing the same marked line is generally the only reasonable option; parties uncomfortable with the exposed, loose 3rd-class terrain should turn around before committing to the gully rather than partway through it.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before entering the crux gully — the terrain is loose, exposed, and route-finding-dependent, and becomes significantly more hazardous if wet, snow-covered, or approached in fading light or building afternoon thunderstorms, which are common in the North Cascades in summer.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September is the consensus best window, once the approach trail and gully are snow-free; some sources extend this into early October in low-snow years. Earlier-season attempts risk lingering snow/ice in the gully and higher, more dangerous creek fords on the approach.
routes.season: Jul-Sep (extending into early Oct in low-snow years)
routes.face: Southeast ridge, approached from the Crater Lake basin on the peak's south side
objHaz: Loose, choss-covered rock in the crux gully and cliff bands near 7,600 ft
Exposed, unroped 3rd-class scrambling with real fall consequences
High-water creek fords (Canyon Creek, Granite Creek) early in the season
Afternoon thunderstorms typical of North Cascades summers
Small, low-density grizzly bear population in the Pasayten Wilderness
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is used on the standard route; it is climbed as an unroped scramble. Some parties carry a light rope/harness for the crux gully as a personal safety margin, particularly for less-experienced scramblers or in wet conditions.
routes.proNeeds: None required for the standard line; it is not a protected/roped route. An ice axe may be prudent in early season if snow lingers in the gully or on the upper slopes.
gear: Map/compass or GPS (route-finding through cliff bands is nontrivial)
Ice axe (early season only, if snow persists)
whatToBring: Wilderness permit (self-issue at Canyon Creek Trailhead)
Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent for trailhead parking
Water treatment (Crater Lake and creek crossings are the water sources)
Bear-aware food storage
Extra layers — exposed ridge and summit are wind-prone
Headlamp given the long day
proTips: Follow the fading yellow paint X's and arrows through the gully closely — going off-line here is the main way parties get cliffed out or hit unnecessarily loose rock.
A helmet is worth carrying given the amount of loose rock ("choss") in the crux section.
Splitting the trip with a camp at Crater Lake meaningfully de-stresses the ~15-mile, ~6,300 ft day for most parties.
watchOut: The 3rd-class gully near 7,600 ft is the crux — loose, exposed, and easy to go off-route on without the painted markers.
Canyon Creek and Granite Creek fords near the trailhead can run high and dangerous in early season/high runoff.
The approach trail is described as brushy and overgrown past the ~5,000 ft junction toward Crater Lake.
knownHazards: Loose rock ("choss") through the crux gully and surrounding cliff bands is the primary objective hazard on this route.
A small grizzly bear population is present in the Pasayten Wilderness; standard bear-aware practices apply.
Early- and high-runoff-season creek fords on the approach can be hazardous.

pitchDetail.notes: Steep switchbacking climb (~55 switchbacks) through timber from Canyon Creek TH (1,900 ft), fording Canyon Creek and Granite Creek, gaining ~3,400 ft over 4 miles to a ~5,000-5,260 ft junction.
~1 mile west on an overgrown user trail to Crater Lake basin (~5,000-5,700 ft) on the peak's south side.
Steep ~400 ft gain from Crater Lake basin onto the SE ridge, moderating near 6,800 ft; traverse around the west side of a false summit.
Loose gully near 7,600 ft with multiple cliff bands; marked by fading yellow paint X's/arrows and cairns. Reported as the route's crux — loose rock and route-finding-dependent.
Scree/talus above the gully leads to the summit plateau and remnants of the old fire lookout at 8,132 ft.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: Open — fully reopened June 14, 2026 after emergency repairs
road.seasonalGate: SR 20 closes seasonally each winter (typically mid-November through spring) at gates near Ross Dam/Diablo Lake and Early Winters; check WSDOT pass status before driving.
road.driveNote: Canyon Creek Trailhead sits directly off SR 20 near milepost 141-142 (~32.8 mi east of Marblemount, ~55.1 mi west of Winthrop) — no additional forest road is needed. A December 2025 atmospheric-river washout severely damaged a 6-mile stretch of SR 20 between the Canyon Creek Trailhead and Granite Creek (mileposts 142-148), including over 1,000 ft of undermined/collapsed pavement, closing the highway from MP 130-156; the trailhead itself was inaccessible during repairs and the highway fully reopened June 14, 2026.

climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy precipitation typical of the North Cascades crest; the Pasayten Wilderness receives significant winter snowpack that lingers into summer at higher elevations.
climate.summer: Best climbing/hiking window; still subject to afternoon thunderstorms common in Cascade summers.
climate.fall: Conditions remain climbable into early October in low-snow years before winter snow returns.
climate.winter: Snowbound and inaccessible; SR 20 itself closes seasonally, cutting off the trailhead.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District (Pasayten Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30/year day-use fee at Canyon Creek Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency America the Beautiful Pass accepted, plus Digital Day Pass).
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead to enter the Pasayten Wilderness. Overnight camping that continues into the adjoining North Cascades National Park Complex (e.g., via the Devils Dome loop) requires a separate paid NPS backcountry permit ($10/person + $6 reservation fee) from the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount — not applicable to a standard out-and-back Crater Mountain trip, which stays on National Forest land.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / Interagency Recreation Pass (or pay the daily fee) at Canyon Creek Trailhead.
access.closures: SR 20 access closes seasonally in winter; the highway (including the trailhead segment) was also closed January-June 2026 for emergency washout repairs, reopening June 14, 2026. Canyon Creek Bridge on the Chancellor/Canyon Creek Trail #754 has separately been reported down, affecting an alternate approach (not the main Jackita Ridge line).

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early-morning start recommended given the roughly 15-mile round trip, ~6,300 ft of gain, and the exposed class 3 gully best tackled before afternoon thunderstorms build.

emergency.county: Whatcom County (summit area); trailhead accessed via SR 20 near the Okanogan/Whatcom-Skagit county lines
emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff non-emergency dispatch: 360-676-6911 (911 for emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA (regional trip-planning/rescue coordination); trail itself managed by the Methow Valley Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Winthrop, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Nearest full-service ERs are west via Marblemount: Skagit Valley Hospital (Level III ED), Mount Vernon, WA, and PeaceHealth United General Medical Center, Sedro-Woolley, WA.
emergency.notes: No cell coverage data confirmed for this route; assume no reliable service along most of the approach and treat as a self-rescue/remote wilderness environment. Trip is entirely on Pasayten Wilderness (USFS) land, not inside North Cascades National Park proper.

blurb: Gunnshy Peak (6,218') is the second-highest summit in the Wild Sky Wilderness of the west-central Washington Cascades, rising above the Skykomish River valley near Baring, WA. Fred Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide refers to it as Jumpoff Ridge — the true high point of a ridge that branches north from the Gunn Peak massif — but it is far better known locally as "Gunnshy Peak" because it, not the taller and mostly-hidden Gunn Peak just 0.7 mile to the east, is the prominent summit visible from Gold Bar, Sultan, Monroe, and Everett. It has no official trail and no documented technical rock routes; the standard line is an unmaintained, brushy climber's path that finishes with Class 2-3 scrambling.
hierarchyNote: Peak identity, coordinates, and elevation check out: name/elevation/location are corroborated across SummitPost, Peakbagger, WTA, and (indirectly) Wikipedia's Gunn Peak article (which gives Gunnshy 1,895 m ≈ 6,218 ft). No conflict found with the given data. One thing worth a human glance: Gunnshy Peak sits in the Wild Sky Wilderness near Baring, on the Skykomish River side of US-2, roughly 20 highway miles west of Stevens Pass summit — it's part of the broader Stevens Pass (US-2) corridor but not the immediate Stevens Pass area itself. If wa_stevens_pass_region elsewhere denotes a tight cluster right around the pass, a Wild Sky Wilderness / Skykomish-valley sub-bucket might fit better; if it's used as a broad US-2 corridor grouping (which is common and reasonable), no change is needed. Not flagging this as an error, just noting it for consistency with sibling peaks.

routes.routeName: Gunnshy Peak Standard Route (Barclay Lake Approach)
objHaz: Loose rock / rockfall on scramble terrain
Dense brush (slide alder, devil's club, salmonberry) obscuring the path
Waterfall/stream gully crossing — slick rock, seasonal high water
Route-finding difficulty (no official trail, occasional flagging only)
Avalanche-prone, icy terrain in winter and spring
Exposed Class 2-3 scrambling on the summit block
routes.overview: Gunnshy Peak (Peak 6218), the second-highest summit in the Wild Sky Wilderness, is reached by an unmaintained climber's path that leaves the Barclay Lake trailhead spur, crosses Barclay Creek, climbs a steep forested headwall and a seasonal waterfall gully, then follows brushy ridge and basin terrain past the Tailgunner/Wing saddle to a short, exposed Class 2-3 scramble on the summit block.
routes.beta: There is no official trail to Gunnshy Peak. From a spur road near the Barclay Lake Trailhead, a faint but generally followable climber's path heads east into the forest and leads most of the way to the summit. Along the way parties cross Barclay Creek (log crossing), climb a steep forested headwall to roughly 3,600 ft, drop into and cross a waterfall/stream gully (dry by late summer, medium-to-large flow possible in winter/spring), work through brush and talus past Point 5,760+ near a saddle around 5,360-5,400 ft below Tailgunner (Peak 5,842') and Wing Peak, then either traverse via the Gunn Lake basin (summer/fall) or bypass a rocky buttress on open slopes (winter/spring) to reach the final Class 2-3 summit scramble. Total round trip is roughly 9 miles with 4,300-5,000+ ft of cumulative elevation gain depending on route-finding and how much of the ridge is regained. The route is non-technical but should not be taken for granted — genuine mountaineering skills (route-finding, brush travel, exposed scrambling) are required.
routes.approach: From Monroe, drive east on US-2 to the town of Baring. Near milepost 41-42, across from a convenience store, turn left (north) onto 635th Place NE. Cross the railroad tracks and continue about 0.3 mile to a junction; keep left on 635th Place NE for another 0.1 mile, where the road becomes Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road). Continue roughly 3.9-4.3 miles of gravel forest road (watch for potholes and rough patches; AWD/4x4 recommended after wet weather) to the Barclay Lake Trailhead (vault toilet on site, parking for roughly 20 vehicles, Northwest Forest Pass required). From a spur road near the trailhead, follow the spur toward an old fire pit, where a faint, mostly-unmarked climber's path heads east into the forest and begins the ascent proper.
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the ascent line — back through the basin/saddle area, across the waterfall gully, and down the steep forested headwall to the Barclay Creek crossing and trailhead spur.
routes.descentText: There is no separate descent route; parties retrace the ascent. The steep, brushy headwall and the waterfall gully crossing are the two sections most worth downclimbing carefully and in daylight — wet rock in the gully and loose footing on the headwall are the main hazards on the way down. In early season or after fresh snow, the open slopes above the saddle and the bypass around Point 5,760+ can be icy or avalanche-prone; reversing this section requires the same care as the ascent.
routes.itinerary: Single-day round trip from the Barclay Lake Trailhead: drive to trailhead, hike the spur road and climber's path via Barclay Creek crossing and the forested headwall, cross the waterfall gully, work along the ridge past the Tailgunner/Wing saddle and Point 5,760+ (via Gunn Lake basin in summer/fall or the open-slope bypass in winter/spring), scramble the summit block, then retrace the entire route back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: There are few clean bail options once past the waterfall gully — the terrain is brushy and cliff-banded off-route. The most straightforward retreat from any point on the route is to reverse the ascent line back down through the basin/saddle, across the waterfall gully, and down the forested headwall to the Barclay Creek crossing and trailhead spur.
routes.turnaround: Because the route has no official trail, requires real route-finding through brush and a seasonal waterfall crossing, and finishes with exposed Class 2-3 scrambling on the summit block, set a firm turnaround time based on your party's pace, the day's weather, and remaining daylight so that the waterfall gully and the steep forested headwall are not downclimbed after dark or in rain.
routes.bestSeason: Optimum months for a summit attempt are July through September. The waterfall/stream gully crossing on route is medium-to-large and can be hazardous in winter and spring, but is typically low or entirely dry by late summer, which is also when terrain is least avalanche-prone and icy — making late summer the most favorable window for most parties.
routes.detailedRack: This is a non-technical scramble route; no roped protection rack is used or needed on the standard line.
routes.proNeeds: No technical protection is placed on the standard route — it is a Class 2-3 scramble typically climbed without a rope. A helmet for rockfall/loose rock, sturdy scrambling boots, and trekking poles for the steep brushy sections are the essential kit rather than any rack.
gear: Map and compass or GPS track (route is unofficial/unmarked)
whatToBring: Helmet for rockfall and loose rock
Trekking poles for the steep, brushy sections
Water treatment — Barclay Creek/the waterfall gully may be the only reliable water source
Map, compass, or a GPS track, since the route is unofficial and only partly flagged
Northwest Forest Pass (or Interagency/digital day-use pass) for trailhead parking
proTips: Occasional flagging/ribbons mark parts of the climber's path, but don't rely on them exclusively — the route is genuinely unmarked in places.
The waterfall gully crossing is easiest and driest in late summer (roughly August-September) when flow is minimal.

Gunnshy Peak is frequently confused with neighboring Gunn Peak (6,240'-6,244', about 0.7 mile east and slightly higher) — the peak visible from Gold Bar, Sultan, Monroe, and Everett is Gunnshy, not Gunn.
Snow can linger in the Gunn Lake basin well into summer; check recent trip reports before committing to the summer/fall (Gunn Lake) variation versus the winter/spring buttress-bypass variation.
watchOut: No official trail exists — expect genuine route-finding through forest, brush, over a ridge, and across a waterfall gully.
The waterfall/stream gully crossing can carry medium-to-large flow and be slick, especially in winter and spring.
Terrain is avalanche-prone and icy in winter and spring conditions.
Dense slide alder, devil's club, and salmonberry can obscure the path and cause parties to lose the route.
The final summit block requires an exposed Class 2-3 scramble with no fixed protection.
knownHazards: Loose rock and exposed scrambling on the summit block.
A seasonal waterfall/creek crossing with slick rock underfoot, worst in winter/spring high water.
Avalanche-prone, icy terrain in winter and spring.
A steep, brushy, largely unmarked climber's path with significant route-finding difficulty.

pitchDetail.notes: Log crossing of Barclay Creek near the trailhead spur; footing can be tricky when the creek is running high.
A steep, faint climber's path climbs through forest to roughly 3,600 ft — described by one trip report as an 'insanely steep start.'
The path drops steeply to cross a narrow waterfall/stream gully around 3,600-4,000 ft; medium-to-large flow is possible in winter/spring, often dry by late summer/fall. Watch for slick rock.
Talus, boulders, heather slopes, and brush (slide alder, devil's club) lead past Point 5,760+ to a saddle around 5,360-5,400 ft near Tailgunner (Peak 5,842') and Wing Peak.

Summer/fall variation descends to the Gunn Lake basin and traverses west before climbing north toward the summit; winter/spring variation instead descends roughly 200 ft around a rocky buttress from the saddle and ascends open slopes northwest to the summit.
A short, exposed scramble on slabs and talus gains the 6,218' summit.

road.name: Forest Road 6024 (Barclay Creek Road)
road.status: Open; gravel, generally passable to passenger cars but with potholes and rough patches
road.driveNote: From US-2 in Baring (near milepost 41-42, across from a convenience store), turn north onto 635th Place NE, cross the railroad tracks, continue about 0.3 mile to a junction, keep left for another 0.1 mile where the road becomes FR-6024, then continue roughly 3.9-4.3 miles of gravel road (watch for potholes; AWD/4x4 recommended after wet weather) to the Barclay Lake Trailhead.

climate.spring: The route's waterfall/stream gully crossing can run medium-to-large, and terrain above the headwall remains avalanche-prone and icy.
climate.summer: Optimum climbing window (July-September); the waterfall gully crossing is typically low or entirely dry.
climate.fall: Waterfall crossing dry and terrain largely snow-free through September, though snow can return by late October.
climate.winter: Avalanche-prone, icy terrain on the upper route; the waterfall/stream gully can run medium-to-large.

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service — Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.fees: $5/vehicle/day or $30/year day-use fee at the Barclay Lake Trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass, Interagency Recreation Pass, or digital day-use pass
access.permit: No wilderness entry permit required; standard Wild Sky Wilderness regulations apply (Leave No Trace, no bikes/stock/motorized use on the trail).

waypoints.name: Ridge Junction (Gunn-Barclay Divide, above waterfall gully)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency dispatch: (425) 407-3999 countywide (or 911 for emergencies/search & rescue)
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288 — (360) 677-2414
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe Emergency Department, 14701 179th Ave SE, Monroe, WA 98272
emergency.notes: Search and rescue response in this area is coordinated by the Snohomish County Sheriff's Office together with Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR).

blurb: Greenwood Mountain is the informally named northeast subpeak of Dumbell Mountain (8,416-8,421 ft), rising with about 625 ft of prominence above their connecting saddle deep in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, above Railroad Creek and the historic mining town of Holden. The peak is built of old, weathered quartz diorite and granitic rock of the Cloudy Pass batholith (~20 million years old); it is one of Washington's "Bulger" peaks (the state's 100 highest, ranked ~74th) and is almost always climbed together with Dumbell via a shared approach from Phelps Creek.

routes.overview: Greenwood Mountain's standard line follows its West Ridge, reached via a long, exposed connecting ledge from neighboring Dumbell Mountain; it is an unroped talus-and-scree scramble almost always climbed together with Dumbell in a single trip staged from upper Phelps Basin.
routes.beta: From a camp in upper Phelps Basin, climb scree/talus below Dumbell's south side to a long, narrow, airy ledge that wraps around Dumbell's east face to the Dumbell-Greenwood col near a 7,900 ft notch on Greenwood's southeast ridge (the final 100 ft to the notch is loose Class 2+). From the notch/col, an easy Class 2 talus walk up the West Ridge leads to the summit, which has a summit register (kept in a Boy Scouts of America can on the south summit). The route is unroped scrambling throughout, but the connecting ledge is in places only about a foot wide with steep exposure, passes under a small overhang requiring a crawl in one spot, and can hold snow and require an ice axe/crampons into mid-August.
routes.approach: From the Phelps Creek Trailhead (3,500 ft, Wenatchee River Ranger District), follow Phelps Creek Trail #1511 north, entering the Glacier Peak Wilderness at 2.6 miles, to Spider Meadow (about 4.5-5 miles from the trailhead). Continue past Spider Meadow on the climbers' path, staying right of the tumbling creek to scramble up slabs past waterfalls, then continue up grass, talus, and heather slopes trending northeast into upper Phelps Basin. A commonly used bivy sits around 5,700-5,800 ft near treeline with stream access (the basin above 6,000 ft often has only snow-patch camping).
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the West Ridge talus to the notch/col, then reverse the exposed connecting ledge along Dumbell's east face and the scree/talus gully back into upper Phelps Basin, retracing the approach to Spider Meadow and the trailhead.
routes.descentText: Descend the West Ridge talus back to the ~7,900 ft notch, then carefully reverse the narrow connecting ledge around Dumbell's east face to the col above the south-side gully. Downclimb the loose Class 2-3 gully/ledges back into upper Phelps Basin; on snow, plunge-stepping or glissading speeds this section when conditions allow. There is no rappel route commonly described for this line — the ledge and gullies are downclimbed. Do not attempt the ledge crossing in the wet; several parties have retreated rather than cross it in rain.
routes.itinerary: Most parties treat this as a 2-3 day trip: Day 1, hike in roughly 5 miles from the Phelps Creek Trailhead to a camp in upper Phelps Basin (~5,700-5,800 ft); Day 2, climb Dumbell and/or Greenwood via the connecting col and ledge (round trips from camp have taken roughly 8-11 hours) and return to camp; Day 3, hike out. Some strong parties have done both summits car-to-car in a single very long day (one recorded example: ~18 miles round trip with about 5,800 ft of cumulative gain, ~10 hours 45 minutes, covering both Dumbell and Greenwood).
routes.bail: Retreat from the connecting ledge back to the Dumbell/Greenwood col and down the scree/talus gully into upper Phelps Basin the way you came; the ledge and gullies are downclimbed in reverse rather than rappelled.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before starting the exposed connecting-ledge traverse if weather is deteriorating, the rock is wet, or the remaining daylight won't cover the round trip back to camp — the ledge's narrow, sloping, foot-wide sections are far more hazardous to reverse in poor visibility, rain, or darkness.
routes.bestSeason: Typically climbed July through September, after the upper basin's snow has mostly melted. Parties climbing through early-to-mid August should expect moderate snow slopes in the upper basin and on the connecting ledge and should carry an ice axe and crampons; by September, snow is largely gone but wildfire smoke has affected visibility in some years. At least one winter ascent has been recorded (February 2023), though winter conditions are not well documented in available trip reports.
objHaz: Loose, weathered rock and talus throughout
Long, narrow, exposed connecting ledge on Dumbell's east face (in places ~1 ft wide with steep drop-offs)
Residual snow/ice on the upper basin and connecting ledge into mid-August
Rockfall potential in the scree/talus gullies below Dumbell's south ridge
Avalanche debris observed mid-meadow on the approach in some years
knownHazards: The connecting traverse ledge between the Dumbell/Greenwood col and Greenwood's west ridge is roughly a foot wide in places with steep exposure to the east, and one section passes under a small overhang that narrows enough to require crawling.
Early-season parties (through mid-August) encounter moderate snow slopes in the upper basin and on the connecting ledge, requiring an ice axe and crampons.
The final 100 feet of the approach to the 7,900-foot notch on Greenwood's southeast ridge is very loose Class 2+ rock.
Wet or rainy conditions have caused parties to retreat from the exposed ledge traverse rather than cross it.
watchOut: Do not attempt the exposed connecting ledge in wet or rainy conditions — reduced friction on this narrow, sloping traverse has turned back parties.
Rockfall risk in the scree/talus gullies below Dumbell's south ridge, especially with other parties above.
Route-finding to the correct 7,900 ft notch is easy to get wrong in poor visibility — aim specifically for the notch on the southeast ridge to the right of Dumbell's summit.
proTips: Camp at roughly 5,700-5,800 ft near treeline in upper Phelps Basin (rather than pushing to the snow-covered basin above 6,000 ft) for a better bivy with stream access.
Climb Dumbell and Greenwood together in one trip via the connecting col/ledge — nearly all parties combine both summits given the shared approach.
Start the ledge traverse early in the day and watch the weather; multiple trip reports describe retreating from or being caught out on the ledge by afternoon rain.

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend scree/talus gully from upper Phelps Basin (~5,700-6,000 ft) toward Dumbell's south ridge and the connecting col.
Long, narrow, exposed ledge on Dumbell's east face; roughly a foot wide in spots, passes under a small overhang requiring a crawl in one section; snow-covered into mid-August.
From the ~7,900 ft notch on the southeast ridge (last 100 ft to the notch is loose Class 2+), walk up talus and scree on the West Ridge to the summit.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) to Phelps Creek Road (FR 6211)
road.status: Chiwawa River Road (FR 6200) is closed to vehicles beyond the Atkinson Flat Campground junction (~mile 16, Order 06-17-07-2026-11) as of May 20, 2026, through at least December 31, 2027, due to December 2025 storm/flood damage, with no reopening estimate. This closure point is before the Phelps Creek Road turnoff (~mile 22), so the Phelps Creek Trailhead is presently inaccessible by vehicle — confirm current status with the Wenatchee River Ranger District before a trip.
road.driveNote: When open: from Coles Corner on US-2, take SR-207 4.2 miles, Chiwawa Loop Road 1.3 miles, then Chiwawa River Road (FR 62) about 22 miles (rough, high-clearance recommended; gravel begins around mile 11), then FR 6211 for 2.3 rough miles to the trailhead at 3,500 ft (~41.7 miles from Leavenworth).

climate.typical: High-elevation North Cascades alpine climate; snow lingers in the upper basin and on the shaded connecting ledge into July and sometimes mid-August.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window for the route with the best chance of a snow-free (or mostly snow-free) ledge traverse; afternoon thunderstorms and rain can still turn parties back from the ledge.
climate.fall: Cooler and typically drier underfoot by September, but wildfire smoke has reduced visibility on the peak in some years, and early-season snow is possible late in the month.
climate.winter: Not well documented in available trip reports; at least one winter ascent has been recorded (February 11, 2023).

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Wenatchee River Ranger District — Glacier Peak Wilderness
access.fees: No fee at the Phelps Creek Trailhead itself

gear: Crampons (through mid-August, or after fresh snow)
whatToBring: Ten Essentials
Overnight/bivy gear for a 2-3 day trip (most parties do not day-trip from the trailhead)
Water treatment (streams in Phelps Basin)
Sun protection for extended talus/snow travel
Map/compass or GPS — the 7,900 ft notch and connecting ledge are easy to miss in poor visibility
routes.detailedRack: This is an unroped scramble, not a technical rope route — no rock rack is needed for the standard West Ridge line. An ice axe and crampons are recommended, and often necessary, for the snow-covered basin and connecting ledge into mid-August. Some parties carry a short rope/harness as a personal safety margin for the exposed ledge traverse, though it is not commonly protected and no fixed anchors are described.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection exists on this route. It is Class 2-3 scrambling with one notably exposed, narrow ledge traverse; parties uncomfortable with unprotected exposure sometimes carry a rope to hand-line the ledge crossing, though most climb it unroped.
routes.corrections: Sources give slightly different summit elevations for Greenwood Mountain: 8,408 ft (Wikipedia/Peakbagger-derived), 8,413 ft (ListsOfJohn — matches this database's existing figure), and 8,415-8,421 ft figures that sometimes conflate it with neighboring Dumbell Mountain (8,416-8,421 ft) in trip-report shorthand. These reflect differing surveys of an informally named subpeak rather than a location conflict; the existing 8,413 ft value is retained here as it matches the most precise single-peak source (ListsOfJohn).

blurb: Golden Horn is an 8,366-foot granite peak in the Okanogan Range of the North Cascades, rising above the Pacific Crest Trail north of Rainy and Washington Pass within the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. It is composed of distinctive rapakivi-textured granite from the roughly 45-million-year-old Golden Horn batholith, and offers both an easy scramble standard route and several rarely-climbed technical rock faces. Fred Beckey, Keith Rankin, and Charles Welsh made the first ascent via the southwest side on September 18, 1946.

routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Keith Rankin, and Charles Welsh — September 18, 1946
routes.rockGrade: Low 5th class (roughly 5.0–5.4); about 10 ft of 5th-class climbing on the summit block
routes.rappels: One short rappel (~40 ft) off the summit block; a 30m rope is sufficient
objHaz: lingering snow on Cutthroat/Granite Pass into early summer
routes.season: Jul–Oct is prime; passable May–Jun with more snow near the summit and on the approach passes
routes.overview: A long, scenic PCT approach to a camp at Snowy Lakes, followed by an easy scree-and-talus scramble that ends in one short, exposed low-5th-class move to the true summit.
routes.beta: The standard and by far most-climbed route on Golden Horn. From camp at Snowy Lakes, climbers ascend loose scree and talus on the mountain's southwest side to the summit ridge, then negotiate a short, exposed low-5th-class step — commonly described as a stem or mantle across a gap — to reach the small summit block. Many parties belay or protect the final move with a short rope despite its brevity, given the exposure below.
routes.approach: Drive SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Rainy Pass (4,880 ft) near milepost 158, about 35 miles west of Winthrop or 51 miles east of Marblemount. Park at the Rainy Pass North (PCT) trailhead. Hike north on the Pacific Crest Trail through forest and switchbacks to Cutthroat Pass (~6,800 ft), continue past Granite Pass (~6.25 miles in, where snowfields can linger into August) and along a cliff-cut trail toward Methow Pass. About a mile before Methow Pass, a signed spur trail switchbacks up to Lower and then Upper Snowy Lake (~6,700–7,000 ft) — roughly 10–11 miles and 2,700 ft of gain one-way from the trailhead. Good campsites sit in the meadow near the lakes.
routes.descent: Reverse the route: a short rappel off the summit block, then downclimb the scree/talus gully back to Snowy Lakes camp and out the PCT.
routes.descentText: From the summit, most parties make a single short rappel (roughly 40 ft; a 30m rope is plenty) off the summit block to regain the ridge, then downclimb/scramble class 2–3 scree and talus back toward Snowy Lakes — plunge-stepping the loose scree speeds this considerably. From camp, retrace the PCT approach back over Granite and Cutthroat Passes to Rainy Pass.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Rainy Pass trailhead, hike ~10–11 miles via the PCT over Cutthroat and Granite Passes to Snowy Lakes, make camp. Day 2: Alpine start from camp, scramble scree/talus to the summit ridge, negotiate the short 5th-class summit block, rappel/downclimb, return to camp, then hike out. Strong parties sometimes complete the whole outing car-to-car in one long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward — the terrain below the final summit block is class 2–3 scree/talus in essentially every direction, so parties can simply downclimb back toward Snowy Lakes from nearly any point below the summit block. The only place rappel gear is genuinely useful is the short step immediately below the true summit.
routes.turnaround: Because the route gains a broad, exposed ridge after a long, largely unshaded approach, turn around with enough daylight and margin to be off the upper mountain and back below treeline before afternoon thunderstorms or a weather change moves in — the PCT approach alone is long enough that an unplanned late descent adds real risk.
routes.bestSeason: Prime conditions run roughly July through October, once seasonal snow has cleared from Cutthroat and Granite Passes and the upper mountain. Late September into early October is the most popular window, when western larches along the PCT turn gold — but this also brings heavy trailhead and camp congestion at Snowy Lakes. May and June ascents are possible but typically require an ice axe (and sometimes crampons) for lingering snow on the passes and near the summit.
routes.detailedRack: Gear is minimal. Most parties carry a short/light rope (a 30m glacier or 'rad' line is common) purely to protect or rappel the short low-5th-class step at the summit block, plus a sling or two for a hasty rappel anchor. An ice axe (and crampons in early season) is recommended for the Cutthroat/Granite Pass snowfields that can linger into August.
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection exists on the route; the crux is short enough that many parties simply spot or hip-belay it. A rope, a sling, and a way to build a quick anchor are enough for those who want to protect or rappel the final move.
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
overnight backpacking/camping gear for Snowy Lakes
water treatment (lake water available at camp)
map/compass or GPS
bear canister recommended for food storage
layers for exposed, high-elevation ridge walking
proTips: Camp at Upper Snowy Lake for the shortest, most direct position below Golden Horn (and Mount Hardy).
If visiting during larch season (late Sept–early Oct), arrive at the trailhead Friday evening — weekend parking fills fast.
The crux move is short but exposed over real air; even confident scramblers often protect it with a quick hip belay or toprope.
Combine with Tower Mountain (and optionally Mount Hardy) from the same Snowy Lakes camp for an efficient multi-summit trip.
watchOut: A short, exposed 'no-fall zone' step onto the true summit — a slip here is consequential despite the low technical grade.
Loose scree and talus on the approach gully can be tedious and prone to kicking rocks onto parties below.
Snowfields on Cutthroat and Granite Pass can persist into August and may require an ice axe.
Groups larger than about 6 increase rockfall risk to each other on the loose gully.
knownHazards: Loose rock and scree are the primary objective hazard on the approach gully and summit block.
The exposed traverse/mantle onto the true summit carries real consequences despite its short length.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common on the exposed upper ridge in summer.

Short, exposed stem/mantle move across a gap to the true summit; often protected with a hip belay or short toprope; downclimbed or rappelled (~40 ft) on descent

objHaz: rarely climbed / little current beta on rock quality or protection
routes.overview: A steep, rarely climbed line up Golden Horn's sheer north face, first climbed by Fred Beckey and Roe 'Duke' Watson in 1958; documented sources give little beta beyond its first-ascent record.
routes.beta: Listed on Mountain Project as a distinct technical alpine route from the southwest standard route, but very little published trip-report detail exists — the north and east faces of Golden Horn are noted in regional sources as sheer and infrequently climbed.
watchOut: Very little modern beta exists for this face — treat rock quality and protection as unknown until on route.
knownHazards: The face is described in regional sources as sheer and rarely climbed, implying unknown/variable rock quality.

routes.overview: A five-pitch, III 5.8 alpine rock route up Golden Horn's northeast arête, first climbed by Gordy Skoog and Jim Walseth in 1979.
routes.beta: A moderate, multi-pitch line following the arête on the mountain's northeast side. Fred Beckey's guidebook description characterizes the rock as looking classic but climbing 'shaky,' with numerous fragile flakes of dubious stability and protection that is sometimes insecure.
routes.approach: Same general Snowy Lakes basecamp approach as the standard route (PCT from Rainy Pass), continuing to the base of the northeast side of the peak; specific route-finding beta beyond this is limited in available sources.
routes.proNeeds: Fred Beckey notes protection can be insecure on fragile flakes — a standard alpine rock rack with an emphasis on small-to-medium cams/nuts and care in placement selection is implied by route descriptions.
watchOut: Fred Beckey describes the arête's rock as shaky despite its classic appearance, with numerous fragile flakes and sometimes-insecure protection.
knownHazards: Loose/fragile rock flakes along the arête, with protection reported as sometimes insecure.

routes.fa: Joe Sambataro and Jason Schilling — June 27, 2015
objHaz: expanding sandy cracks with poor protection
routes.overview: An 11-pitch, roughly 1,000-foot IV 5.10+ R alpine rock route up Golden Horn's east face, climbed ground-up and onsight with no bolts or fixed gear by its first ascensionists in June 2015. The first-ascent party humorously nicknamed the line after a comedic song title; the face has a reputation for inconsistent rock quality.
routes.beta: The route's crux is a technical 5.10+ third pitch on comparatively good rock, followed by a harder and more dangerous fourth-pitch 'true crux' — an expanding, sandy crack with poor protection and disintegrating holds (5.10 R). A roughly 20-foot runout section over extremely rotten rock earned the nickname 'the Pitch of Terror.' Bands of loose, decomposing 'kitty litter' rock alternate with sections of solid granite throughout the face.
routes.approach: From a camp at Snowy Lakes (reached via Swamp Creek and the PCT from Rainy Pass), the first-ascent party scrambled the ridge and traversed a scree field on the mountain's southwest side, then descended to the base of the east face before roping up.
routes.descent: A snow couloir on the east side provides a straightforward descent with crampons and an ice axe.
routes.descentText: The first-ascent party descended via a snow couloir on Golden Horn's east side, described as a straightforward descent given crampons and an axe, back toward the Snowy Lakes basin.
routes.detailedRack: A standard alpine trad rack for sustained 5.10 crack and face climbing; no bolts or fixed gear exist anywhere on the route, so a full self-sufficient rack (including small-to-mid cams for the sandy, expanding crux crack) plus a helmet is essential.
routes.proNeeds: The route has no fixed protection or bolts; the crux fourth pitch in particular offers poor, marginal placements in an expanding sandy crack, and one ~20-foot section ('the Pitch of Terror') is essentially unprotectable rotten rock.
gear: crampons and ice axe for the descent couloir
whatToBring: overnight camping gear for a Snowy Lakes basecamp
extra water for a long, exposed east-facing pitch day
proTips: This is a serious, committing outing — the first ascent took 11 hours for 11 pitches with an early alpine start and a late-evening summit; budget a full day and a headlamp margin.
watchOut: Expect loose, disintegrating holds and sandy, expanding cracks on multiple pitches — this is not a route to expect clean granite throughout.
No bolts or fixed protection exist anywhere on the route; it was first climbed ground-up and onsight.
knownHazards: The east face has a reputation for inconsistent, sometimes rotten rock quality ('kitty litter').
A roughly 20-foot runout section over extremely rotten rock (nicknamed 'the Pitch of Terror') offers essentially no protection.

pitchDetail.notes: Technical crux on comparatively good rock; tricky transition from crack climbing to slopers
The route's true crux — an expanding, sandy crack with poor protection and disintegrating 'kitty litter' holds
Another loose section requiring careful protection placement
Nicknamed 'the Pitch of Terror' — extremely rotten, essentially unprotectable rock

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start from Snowy Lakes camp (first ascent began climbing around 6:30am after a 4:30am camp departure)

blurb: Big Four Mountain (6,160-6,180 ft, most commonly cited as ~6,170 ft) rises above the Mountain Loop Highway in the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, about 20 miles east of Granite Falls and 4 miles west of Barlow Pass in Snohomish County, Washington. It is built of pre-Tertiary metasedimentary rock (including friable Swauk-Chuckanut sandstone and conglomerate) uplifted via terrane accretion roughly 50 million years ago and later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation. The peak is best known for its imposing 4,000-foot North Face and the permanent, low-elevation ice caves at its base -- a hazardous feature that has collapsed fatally on hikers in 1998, 2010, and 2015 and is now permanently off-limits. Big Four was first climbed July 19, 1931 by Forest Farr and Art Winder via the northwest ridge; it remains one of the most rugged, dangerous, and infrequently repeated technical objectives on the Mountain Loop Highway corridor, with no genuinely easy route to the summit.

routes.overview: Big Four's standard/easiest line: a long, steep approach up the Dry Creek basin on the mountain's east side, followed by a gully and an exposed traverse of red slabs to the east ridge, then the summit ridge to the true (higher, second) summit bump. There is no easy way up this peak -- even the 'standard' route involves sustained class 3-4 scrambling, a committing couloir, and a technical rappel/downclimb on the way out.
routes.approach: From the Big Four Picnic Area / Ice Caves Trailhead (1,640 ft) on the Mountain Loop Highway, follow the paved nature-trail toward the ice caves, then leave the trail to cross the South Fork Stillaguamish River on a fallen log. Travel southeasterly through open forest roughly a mile to reach Dry Creek, then ascend through the lower basin -- in snow conditions via a hidden snow chute, in bare conditions via a series of class 3-4 ledges and ramps -- into the upper basin. From the upper basin, traverse the exposed red slabs to gain the east ridge below the couloir/gully system that leads to the summit ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the Dry Creek line: from the summit follow the summit ridge back, then rappel (~60m) from a notch on the south ridge before downclimbing steep terrain back into the lower basin.
routes.descentText: Most parties descend the same Dry Creek line they climbed. From the true summit, reverse the summit ridge over the lower (first) bump, then set up a roughly 60-meter rappel from a notch on the south ridge -- some parties have found a 60m rope barely reaches the anchor/chockstone. Below the rappel, bypass steep snow and waterfall-moistened rock to the side, then downclimb roughly two more rope-lengths of steep terrain to regain the lower basin before retracing the approach back across the South Fork Stillaguamish and out to the trailhead. In winter/spring, parties instead downclimb the Dry Creek couloir itself, which multiple trip reports describe as a genuinely dangerous, insecure downclimb.
routes.bail: Below the red-slab traverse, the lower basin/gully offers the only reasonable walk-off retreat. Once above the traverse on the east or summit ridge, retreat generally requires reversing the same technical terrain (the south-ridge rappel/downclimb) rather than a true alternate bail-out -- commit only with enough daylight and party strength to reverse the crux.
routes.turnaround: Because the descent requires a technical rappel and exposed downclimbing rather than a simple walk-off, turn around with enough daylight and party capability remaining to reverse the crux terrain before dark, and before afternoon warming destabilizes snow in the couloir on spring ascents.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed in two very different modes: as a snow/ice climb up the Dry Creek couloir in winter and early spring (roughly December-April) when the gully is filled in, or as a rock scramble in summer/early fall once the basin's ledges and ramps have melted out. Guidebook sources warn that the exposed rock is loose and decomposing once bare, while winter/spring ascents carry significant avalanche exposure in the couloir -- there is no genuinely low-risk season on this route.
routes.season: Dec-Apr as a snow/ice couloir climb; Jul-Sep as a rock scramble once snow-free
routes.aspect: NE-facing couloir, with the upper route trending onto the East Face/ridge
routes.rockGrade: Class 3-4 scrambling (sustained class 4 on the lower-basin ledges/ramps and the red-slab traverse)
routes.rappels: One ~60m rappel from a notch on the south ridge during descent

pitchDetail.notes: Log crossing of the South Fork Stillaguamish River, then travel through open forest to the Dry Creek basin
Series of ledges and ramps in the lower basin; deep moats with thin, unsupportive snow overhangs possible where snow meets rock
Exposed traverse of red slabs to reach the east ridge
Follow the summit ridge over the lower (first) bump to the true summit (second, higher bump)

routes.detailedRack: A light rock rack plus a 60m rope for the descent rappel; full steep-snow kit (ice axe, crampons) for winter/spring couloir conditions.
routes.proNeeds: Protection is minimal and often not encountered until the descent rappel anchor; the route is more about steep, exposed scrambling/snow-climbing than sustained gear placements.

GPS and backup map -- route-finding through the basin is reported as difficult even by experienced parties
Full rope length margin for the south-ridge rappel
Insect protection (wasp nests reported in the lower basin)
proTips: Best late-summer conditions come once seasonal snow has melted off the basin's ledges and ramps, exposing the class 4 rock beneath
In snowy conditions the northernmost gully is reported as the preferred winter line
Carry a full 60m rope for the descent rappel -- parties have found it barely reaches the anchor
Check NWAC's West Slopes North zone forecast before committing to the Dry Creek couloir in winter or spring
watchOut: Deep moats with thin, unsupportive snow overhangs form where the gully meets exposed rock
Natural avalanches have been observed releasing in the Dry Creek couloir during winter/spring ascents
The descent rappel/downclimb off the south ridge is technical and has been described by climbers as one of the more harrowing parts of the route
Rock throughout is loose, decomposing metasedimentary/sandstone-conglomerate
knownHazards: Avalanche hazard in the Dry Creek couloir, especially on winter/spring snow climbs
Rockfall from loose, decomposing rock
Deep moats and thin snow overhangs where snow meets rock in the lower basin
Technical, exposed downclimbing/rappel required on descent -- this is not a simple walk-off

objHaz: ice/rockfall from the North Face ice caves area
routes.itinerary: A single long day from the trailhead: hike the approach trail/road to the Dry Creek basin, ascend the gully/ledges and red-slab traverse to the east ridge, follow the summit ridge to the true summit, then reverse via the south-ridge rappel and downclimb back to the basin and trailhead.
routes.beta: The most commonly climbed and most-described line to the summit, but every source treats it as a serious, committing objective rather than a straightforward scramble -- 'no route is easy' on Big Four. Historic trip reports document parties turning back after finding natural avalanche activity in the couloir, and describe the final downclimb as frightening even for experienced climbers.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascades climate; west-facing slopes catch heavy Pacific frontal precipitation, and the North Face builds a substantial seasonal snowpack that feeds the ice caves at its base.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; the Dry Creek couloir is commonly climbed as a spring snow climb, but avalanche hazard is a primary concern.
climate.summer: The rock sections of the route are typically climbed in this window once snow has melted off the ledges, though guidebook sources warn the exposed rock is loose and decomposing.
climate.fall: A brief window of more stable weather before the first significant snow returns to the high peaks of the Mountain Loop Highway corridor.
climate.winter: Heavy snowfall and high avalanche danger; the couloir is climbed as a snow/ice route in this season by parties experienced in avalanche terrain.

routes.overview: A technical rock-and-snow line on the mountain's north side/NE ridge that climbs a prominent gully to the first of three rock towers, works over and between the towers via rock climbing and a rappel into a notch, then finishes up a long, steep, exposed snowfield to the summit.
routes.beta: First climbed July 25, 1972 by Ben Guydelkon and Ron Miller as a new line on Big Four's north side. From the end of the ice-caves trail, the party traversed to the northeast side of the first tower to a prominent gully; climbing this gully let them work west onto and up the first tower. They rappelled into a notch, climbed two more towers, then finished with rock scrambling leading to a long, steep, exposed snowfield to the summit.
routes.approach: Approach via the Big Four Ice Caves trail from the Big Four Picnic Area / Ice Caves Trailhead, then traverse toward the north side of the mountain to reach the base of the first tower's gully.
routes.bail: A rappel into the notch between the first and second towers is required as part of the route itself; below that, retreat generally means reversing the gully system rather than a distinct bail line.
routes.turnaround: Turn around with time and rope in hand to complete the notch rappel and reverse the tower gullies before dark -- this is not a route to be caught on after nightfall.
routes.bestSeason: Climbed in summer conditions (the first ascent was in late July); rock quality on the towers is reported as loose in places, typical of the peak's friable metasedimentary rock.
routes.rockGrade: 5.7 (originally rated F7 under the old NCCS/Class 6 system)
routes.rappels: One rappel into the notch between the first and second towers

pitchDetail.notes: Ascend the prominent gully on the NE side to the first tower
Work west onto and up the first tower
Rappel into the notch between towers
Continue climbing the remaining two towers
Long, steep, exposed snowfield leads to the summit

routes.detailedRack: A moderate rock rack to 5.7, plus rappel gear for the inter-tower notch and an ice axe/crampons for the summit snowfield.
routes.proNeeds: Standard trad rack sufficient for 5.7 terrain on the towers; the crux is more about route-finding and commitment across three towers than sustained hard climbing.
whatToBring: Helmet (loose rock on the towers)
Extra rappel tat/anchor material for the notch
proTips: Expect loose rock typical of Big Four's metasedimentary/sandstone-conglomerate geology -- test holds carefully

Loose rock on the towers themselves

Exposure on the long, steep summit snowfield

routes.overview: A serious ice/mixed line up the central couloir system of Big Four's North Face, climbing névé and water ice through a bowl and headwall, with a near-vertical ice crux around three-quarters height, to mixed drytooling terrain and cornices just below the summit ridge.
routes.beta: First climbed March 2, 1996 by Bart Pauli and Doug Littauer in a fast, ultralight, ~9-hour push with no bivy gear. The route starts up an 'ice ribbon' on the initial slabs (roughly 75-degree névé and water ice), continues through the main couloir's three steps of mostly moderate terrain, and hits its crux about three-quarters height: a 100-foot pitch of near-vertical ice leading to steep, often runnelled snow. The upper section mixes rotten ice, shale, and drytooling moves up to roughly 95-degree angles before summit cornices, which may require tunneling through.
routes.approach: Walk the nearly wheelchair-accessible nature trail from the Big Four Picnic Area toward the Ice Caves, to the open basin below the North Face. From the trail's end, move right to find a break in the lower cliffs -- straightforward with adequate snow cover, but may require bushwhacking on the far right in sparse-snow years.
routes.descent: Descend northwest via rappels and steep downclimbing to the col between Big Four and Hall Peak, then continue with tree-anchored rappels down through the north face bowl to the base.
routes.descentText: The route tops out on the summit ridge well west of the true summit. Descent goes northwest, via rappels and steep downclimbing, to the col between Big Four and Hall Peak; from there, multiple tree-anchored rappels lead back down through the North Face bowl to the base of the face. Careful navigation is essential -- parties have mistakenly descended south instead of northwest in poor visibility.
routes.bail: Below the crux ice pitch, the couloir can generally be downclimbed/rappelled back toward the basin; above the crux, retreat is more difficult and the intended northwest descent toward the Big Four-Hall Peak col becomes the more practical option.
routes.turnaround: Given the couloir's namesake spindrift/avalanche hazard and the technical, multi-rappel descent, turn around with enough time, ice, and daylight remaining to complete the descent to the Big Four-Hall Peak col before conditions deteriorate.
routes.bestSeason: A winter route, with the best conditions typically arriving after a period of warm weather brings rain to the summit followed by a hard freeze, which bonds the snow and ice into more solid névé.
routes.season: Winter, typically after a rain-to-freeze cycle
routes.rappels: Multiple rappels on descent: steep downclimbing/rappels to the Big Four-Hall Peak col, then several tree-anchored rappels through the North Face bowl

routes.detailedRack: Full technical ice/mixed rack: ice screws, rock protection for the mixed upper section, v-threads or similar for rappel anchors, tree slings for the descent through the North Face bowl.
routes.proNeeds: Protection on the upper mixed pitches is reported as marginal (rotten ice, shale); the crux ice pitch takes standard ice screw protection.
whatToBring: Light/fast kit -- the first ascent party climbed with no bivy gear in a single ~9-hour push
Extra webbing/cord for tree-anchored rappels in the descent bowl
proTips: Best conditions come after a warm/rain spell followed by a hard freeze, which bonds the névé
The approach trail is nearly wheelchair-accessible, but travel light and fast once above the basin
A sub-day, ultralight push (as on the first ascent) is the style most commonly described for this route
watchOut: Parties have mistakenly descended south in poor visibility -- the correct descent goes northwest to the Big Four-Hall Peak col
Cornices near the top may require tunneling through
Rock and ice quality deteriorate (rotten ice, shale) through the upper mixed section
Signs warn of serious avalanche hazard on this face in winter
knownHazards: Avalanche and spindrift hazard, for which the route is named
Rockfall/icefall potential off the North Face

Marginal protection on rotten ice/shale in the upper section

routes.itinerary: Typically climbed car-to-car or as a single long push: approach the basin below the North Face, climb the couloir in roughly 9 hours (first-ascent pace) to the summit ridge, then descend northwest via rappels/downclimbing to the Big Four-Hall Peak col and tree-anchored rappels through the bowl.

routes.beta: Big Four Mountain's first ascent was made July 19, 1931 by Forest Farr and Art Winder via the northwest ridge -- the original route to the summit. As with the mountain's other lines, this route begins from the Big Four viewpoint/ice caves trail approach on the Mountain Loop Highway.
routes.approach: Begins from the Big Four viewpoint / Ice Caves Trailhead on the Mountain Loop Highway, as with the peak's other technical routes.

knownHazards: General objective hazards typical of Big Four: loose, decomposing metasedimentary rock and steep, exposed terrain

blurb: Fortune Peak (7,382 ft) is a scramble summit in the Teanaway high country of the Wenatchee Mountains (Cascade Range), sitting on the Kittitas/Chelan county line inside the Alpine Lakes Wilderness about 0.7 mile south-southwest of Ingalls Peak. Its Jurassic-age rock was uplifted during late-Eocene terrane accretion and later sculpted by Pleistocene glaciation; it is unofficially named for its position at the head of Fortune Creek, site of the historic Queen of the Hills gold mine and a nearby antimony/silver mine. It is the second-highest point in the Teanaway area and is most often climbed as an easy, non-technical scramble, frequently combined with neighboring South or North Ingalls Peak in a single day.

routes.routeName: Standard Route (Esmeralda Basin – Lake Ann Pass)
routes.overview: A straightforward off-trail scramble up Fortune Peak's ridge from the pass above Lake Ann, reached via the Esmeralda Basin and Ingalls Way trails; considered the peak's easiest and most commonly climbed line.
routes.beta: Hike the Esmeralda Basin Trail to its junction with the Ingalls Way/Lake Ann trail, continue toward Lake Ann up to the pass/saddle above the lake, then leave the trail and follow an established climbers' boot path along the ridge to the summit.
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda Trailhead (#1394, 4,269 ft), hike the Esmeralda Basin Trail (shared with the Lake Ingalls/Ingalls Way trail for the first 0.4 mi), then continue on the Ingalls Way trail toward Lake Ann. At the trail junction below the pass, head up toward Lake Ann and the saddle above it, then leave maintained trail and follow the ridge boot path to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ridge boot path back down to the pass above Lake Ann, then descend via the Ingalls Way/Esmeralda Basin Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: The drop from the summit ridge back down to the pass above Lake Ann is steep and, especially early in the season, can remain snow-covered well into summer — downclimb carefully or glissade only after checking the runout. Below the pass, rejoin the maintained Ingalls Way/Esmeralda Basin Trail for the hike back to the Esmeralda Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically a single long day: drive to the Esmeralda Trailhead, hike up the Esmeralda Basin Trail to the Ingalls Way junction, continue to the pass above Lake Ann, scramble the ridge to the summit, then reverse the entire route. Many parties combine it with South Ingalls Peak.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward at essentially any point on the ridge by downclimbing back to the pass above Lake Ann and rejoining the maintained trail network.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets the party off the exposed summit ridge and below the pass before afternoon thunderstorms, wind, or deteriorating weather move in — the ridge has no shelter.
routes.bestSeason: Best from July through early October once the pass and ridge have shed most snow. Esmeralda Basin's wildflowers peak in July–August, and larches in the basin turn gold in late September–mid October, a popular time to climb.
objHaz: Loose rock on the ridge scramble
Exposed, wind-swept summit ridge with no shelter
Steep, sometimes snow-covered descent gully to the Lake Ann pass early season
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock gear is needed for the standard scramble line. Carry an ice axe (and crampons if snow lingers on the pass/ridge, common into early summer) — this is not a roped climb for most parties.
gear: Map/compass or GPS for the off-trail ridge section
whatToBring: Ten Essentials
Sun protection
Wind layer for the exposed summit ridge
Plenty of water — none reliable past Esmeralda Basin
proTips: Frequently combined with South Ingalls Peak for a bigger day out of the same trailhead
The ridge boot path is well established in summer/fall but easy to lose under early-season snow
Summit views take in Mount Stuart and the Ingalls Peaks, especially good with fall larch color
watchOut: The gully/ridge down to the Lake Ann pass is steep and can hold snow into midsummer
Esmeralda Trailhead parking fills early — it's shared with heavy Lake Ingalls/Mount Stuart traffic
Some direct lines involve loose, exposed scrambling (T4, approaching T5 on the Mountaineers' scramble scale)
knownHazards: Loose, rocky scrambling terrain along the summit ridge
Afternoon thunderstorms and strong wind exposure on the open ridge
Lingering steep snow near the Lake Ann pass in early season increases fall risk without an axe

routes.overview: An alternate line up Fortune Peak's east side from Headlight Basin below Ingalls Pass; ascends moderate slopes (snow early season, talus/scree later) directly to the summit ridge.
routes.beta: From Ingalls Pass, traverse into Headlight Basin beneath the east side of the Fortune/Ingalls ridge and climb moderately steep slopes — firm snow in early summer, loose talus/scree after melt-out — to gain the summit ridge, then follow it a short distance to the top.
routes.approach: From the Esmeralda Trailhead, hike the Esmeralda Basin Trail and Ingalls Way Trail up to Ingalls Pass (~6,500 ft), then traverse into Headlight Basin below the Ingalls/Fortune ridge.
routes.descent: Downclimb or glissade the east-facing ascent slopes back into Headlight Basin (snow conditions permitting), then retrace over Ingalls Pass and out the Esmeralda Basin Trail.
routes.descentText: Parties climbing while snow still covers the slope (typically into late June) often descend by glissade — roughly 1,000 ft of glissading has been reported back toward camp. Once the slope has melted out later in summer it becomes loose talus/scree requiring careful downclimbing. From the base of the slope, reverse through Headlight Basin, over Ingalls Pass, and back down the Esmeralda Basin Trail.
routes.itinerary: Often combined with North or South Ingalls Peak: Esmeralda Trailhead to Ingalls Pass, into Headlight Basin (sometimes camping there), up the east slope to the Fortune Peak summit, then reverse or continue to an adjacent peak before returning.
routes.bail: The ascent slope is a simple reversal at any point back down into Headlight Basin — no technical anchors are needed to retreat.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before early-season snow on the ascent slope softens in the afternoon sun, which raises rockfall/wet-slide risk and makes the glissade descent unpredictable.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through September. In late June the slope below the summit ridge is often still snow-covered (crampons useful); by August it is mostly a talus/scree scramble.

Loose talus/scree once slopes melt out

blurb: Mount Persis (5,464 ft) is a mostly forested peak in the Central-West Cascades of Snohomish County, Washington, rising directly west of Mount Index above the Skykomish River valley near the town of Index, within Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. It is built of volcanic rock roughly 38 million years old, among the earliest expressions of Cascade volcanism, and while its flanks are shapeless and heavily timbered, its north side drops away in a sheer face that hosts a difficult technical alpine rock route. The peak was named for Persis Gunn (1846-1898), wife of Index founder Amos Gunn, and was first climbed in 1917 by a party led by Harry B. Hinman.
hierarchyNote: Identity/elevation/coordinates check out (5,464 ft matches Wikipedia and WTA exactly; given lat/lng 47.7904,-121.6118 is close to and consistent with sourced trailhead/summit coordinates). One soft geographic note: Mount Persis sits in the Mount Index/Skykomish-River corridor near the town of Index, roughly 15-18 road miles west of Stevens Pass itself. It is on the same US-2 corridor and the "wa_stevens_pass_region" bucket is a plausible simplified grouping for that corridor, but the peak is geographically closer to Index/Gold Bar than to Stevens Pass proper — worth a look if a more specific "Index area" region bucket exists or gets created.

routes.overview: The standard, non-technical route to Mount Persis's summit: a steep boot path that gains the peak's west ridge from Forest Road 62 and follows it east, past Point 5017 (NW Persis) and two small ponds, to the true summit.
routes.beta: A short (about 2 miles one-way, ~2,650-2,700 ft gain) but demanding hike/scramble rated Trail + Class 2 (YDS). More bootpath than maintained trail — it disappears intermittently in brush and undergrowth and is steep enough in spots to require grabbing nearby trees for assistance. The first mile carries most of the elevation gain through young forest and clearcuts into older cedar/Douglas fir, then the path levels onto the ridge, crosses talus fields, dips ~100 ft past Point 5017, and climbs past two small ponds/lakes to the summit for 360-degree views of Mount Index, Mount Baker, and Mount Rainier. Climbable year-round; popular as a winter snowshoe objective when the access road is passable.
routes.approach: From US Highway 2 near Index, turn onto Forest Road 62 (also called No Name Creek Road / Proctor Creek Road / Cavanaugh Lake Road). Follow FR-62 about 3.4-3.5 miles to the first junction (~1,600-1,650 ft) and bear left. Continue about 1 mile to a second junction (~2,380 ft) and bear left again. Drive roughly another 0.7-1 mile of increasingly rough road to its end (~2,800 ft), where there is a small pullout/parking area. An unmarked, steep, well-trodden gully directly opposite the pullout marks the boot-path trailhead. Note: FR-62 crosses private industrial timberland and its gate at the highway is frequently locked at the owner's discretion — some parties have had to walk or e-bike an additional ~5+ miles and ~2,500 ft of road to reach this trailhead.
routes.descent: Descend the same West Ridge boot path back to the FR-62 trailhead.
routes.descentText: Retrace the ascent route: from the summit, drop back past the two ponds, re-climb the ~100 ft notch by Point 5017, and follow the ridge boot path back down through the talus and timber to the FR-62 road-end trailhead. No technical descent/rappels are required on this route in summer conditions; in winter/spring, descend with the same snow travel gear (microspikes/ice axe) used on the way up.
routes.itinerary: Single-day, car-to-car outing: drive FR-62 to the road-end trailhead (allow extra time/mileage if the highway gate is locked), climb the West Ridge boot path to the summit (roughly 2-3 hours of climbing per SummitPost), enjoy summit views, then descend the same way. Total car-to-car time is commonly reported as 4.5+ hours given the steepness and route-finding, longer in winter conditions or if the road gate forces an extended approach.
routes.bail: The route has no technical sections, so retreat is possible at any point by simply turning around and following the boot path/ridge back down to FR-62; no rappels or fixed retreat points are needed on the standard line.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before dark or before deteriorating weather (rain, fog, or fresh snow) obscures the intermittent boot path across the ridge's talus and heather sections, since route-finding is already difficult in good visibility.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable year-round given favorable road access. Most parties go June-October once winter snow has cleared from the upper talus and ridge ponds and the private-timberland gate is more reliably open. Winter ascents are a popular snowshoe objective, but require checking current FR-62 gate status, expect lingering snow/ice, and may add significant road mileage if the highway gate is locked.
routes.season: Jun-Oct typical; year-round with snow travel gear
routes.aspect: W (ridge route); summit views open N/E toward Mount Index
routes.fa: First recorded ascent 1917, party led by Harry B. Hinman (peak first ascent, not specific to this named route)
gear: Microspikes or light ice axe (spring/winter conditions)
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS track (bootpath is easy to lose)
Extra water — little reliable water on route
Bug protection for brushy sections in summer
Layers for exposed ridge/talus sections
proTips: Check current Hampton Resources (formerly Weyerhaeuser) gate/permit status before driving in — FR-62 access at Highway 2 changes without notice and is frequently locked.
Avoid the southwest approach from the head of Proctor Creek; SummitPost describes its brush as 'thicker than the bristles of a paint brush.'
The short mileage is misleading — budget 4.5+ hours car-to-car given the steepness and route-finding.
watchOut: The route is more bootpath than maintained trail and vanishes intermittently in brush, talus, and undergrowth.
Steep sections require using nearby trees for handholds.
FR-62's gate at Highway 2 is frequently locked, which can add 5+ miles and ~2,500 ft of road walking/biking to the day.
Post-holing risk through the boulder field when winter/spring snow cover is patchy.
knownHazards: Dense brush and undergrowth obscure the boot path in multiple sections.
Loose talus along the ridge traverse near Point 5017.
A locked private-timberland gate can force a much longer approach on short notice.
Lingering snow/ice on the upper ridge and around the summit ponds into early summer.

road.name: Forest Road 62 (No Name Creek Road / Proctor Creek Road / Cavanaugh Lake Road)
road.status: Variable — a locked gate near Highway 2 is common; road is otherwise a rough, sometimes rutted, single-lane logging road to the trailhead pullout.
road.seasonalGate: Gate at the Highway 2 end is frequently closed by the private timberland owner (Hampton Resources, formerly Weyerhaeuser), reportedly more often locked than open in recent seasons; status can change with no notice.
road.driveNote: From US-2 near Index, follow FR-62 ~3.4-3.5 mi to the first junction and bear left, then ~1 mi to a second junction and bear left again, then ~0.7-1 mi to the road-end trailhead pullout (~2,800 ft).

climate.spring: Snow lingers on the upper ridge and around the summit ponds into June; brush is wet and route-finding harder.

climate.fall: Wet weather returns by October; road and brush conditions deteriorate.
climate.winter: Heavy snow makes this a popular snowshoe objective, but FR-62 is frequently unplowed/gated, and avalanche/route-finding hazard increases.
