routes.overview: A difficult, multi-pitch technical alpine rock climb up Mount Persis's sheer north face, established by prolific North Cascades first-ascentionist Bryan Burdo.
routes.beta: Documented only briefly (via Mountain Project's area description) as a 'difficult Bryan Burdo alpine rock climb' on Persis's north face — no detailed grade, pitch count, or topo is available from the primary sources checked. This is a serious, low-traffic line distinct from the peak's Class 2 West Ridge hiking route; treat it as an alpine rock objective requiring full trad rack, rope, and North Cascades route-finding/approach skills rather than a hiking route.
routes.fa: Bryan Burdo (party); exact year not documented in the sources checked
watchOut: Very sparse documentation exists for this route — treat it as a serious, low-beta objective and expect route-finding and condition uncertainty typical of an obscure North Cascades alpine rock line.

blurb: Hadley Peak (~7,513–7,522 ft) is a horn-shaped, low-prominence (~370–400 ft) summit in the Skagit Range of the North Cascades, sitting on Mount Baker's north flank where Chowder Ridge meets Dobbs Cleaver, within the Mount Baker Wilderness of the Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. It is composed of the same andesitic volcanic rock typical of the Mount Baker massif and is reached as an off-trail Class 2 (briefly Class 3) alpine scramble rather than a roped technical climb, via either the Skyline Divide or Cougar Divide ridge trails; no documented first-ascent party or date appears in available climbing literature.
hierarchyNote: Confirmed as a real, identifiable summit: Hadley Peak sits on Mount Baker's north ridge/Chowder Ridge complex in the Mount Baker Wilderness, Whatcom County, WA — geographically consistent with a "Shuksan/Baker neighbors" parent grouping (it lies between Mount Baker and Mount Shuksan). The given coordinates (48.814065, -121.822285) closely match independent sources (PeakVisor: 48.813837, -121.822501). Elevation shows normal minor cross-source spread: PeakVisor/community trip-report consensus clusters at 7,513–7,516 ft (2,290–2,291 m), while one source (FastestKnownTime) gives 7,415 ft; the catalog's 7,522 ft is within a plausible rounding/older-datum range of this cluster and is not clearly wrong, so it was left unchanged — flagging only for awareness, not as an error.

routes.overview: The more popular of Hadley Peak's two standard approaches, following the well-known Skyline Divide trail up onto the ridge crest and then continuing cross-country along Chowder Ridge to the base of Hadley's summit block for a long, scenic ridge-walk scramble.
routes.beta: From the Skyline Divide Trailhead, a rugged, eroded trail switchbacks through forest and into alpine meadows for about 4.4–4.5 miles to the first ridge-crest viewpoint (~6,600 ft). From there the maintained tread fades into a boot-packed path that continues along the open ridge crest for roughly another 3 miles, crossing minor ups and downs on Chowder Ridge, before reaching the base of Hadley Peak's final scramble. The last push is Class 2 with a short Class 3 step, climbing loose scree and talus to the summit; exposure is generally minimal for experienced parties.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, drive Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) east about 1 mile past the Glacier Public Service Center to Glacier Creek Road (FS 39); turn right, then take an immediate left onto Deadhorse Road (FS 37). FS 37 normally continues 12.7 miles to the Skyline Divide Trailhead. As of the Forest Service's current alert, FS 37 is impassable at milepost 3.1 due to 2021 flood damage with no estimated repair date, so the trailhead is not currently reachable by vehicle. When open, hike Skyline Divide Trail #678 through old-growth forest switchbacks and into meadows (~4.4–4.5 mi) to the ridge crest, then continue cross-country along the open ridge to Chowder Ridge and the base of Hadley Peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent: downclimb the loose summit scramble back onto the ridge crest, then follow Chowder Ridge and the Skyline Divide trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, carefully downclimb the short Class 3 step and the loose scree/talus below it back to the base of the summit block. Retrace the boot-packed path along Chowder Ridge and the open ridge crest (roughly 3 miles) back to the first viewpoint (~6,600 ft), then descend the maintained Skyline Divide Trail #678 through meadows and forest switchbacks to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Typically done as a long single-day car-to-camp-free outing (when the trailhead is reachable): early trailhead start, roughly 4–5 hours to the summit along the ridge, and a similar time back down; some parties break it into an overnight with a ridge-camp on Chowder Ridge given the length (~14–15 miles round trip).
routes.bail: The ridge route can be abandoned at nearly any point by simply retracing the ridge crest and trail back toward the Skyline Divide Trailhead; there is no fixed rappel or technical retreat required.
routes.turnaround: Because the final scramble involves loose rock and a short exposed step, parties should set a turnaround time that leaves ample daylight and margin for slower downclimbing, and should not push onto the summit block in wet, icy, or low-visibility conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Best from mid-summer through early fall once snow has cleared from the ridge and ambient mosquito pressure has dropped; the trail itself is usually snow-free by July, but lingering snow on the ridge traverse and lower-elevation mosquitoes in early season are common complaints in trip reports.
whatToBring: Recreation/day-use pass or annual Interagency pass for the trailhead
self-issue wilderness permit from the trailhead box
extra water/treatment (little to no reliable water on the ridge)
layers for exposed, wind-prone ridge walking
sun protection
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection or rope is used on the standard scramble line; the main 'gear' concern is footing on loose rock and residual snow, for which an ice axe is recommended in early or late season.
proTips: The ridge route is long and mostly unshaded — start early to beat both heat and afternoon thunderstorm risk on the exposed crest.
Trip reports describe the trail fading into a boot-path partway along Chowder Ridge; carry a GPS track or map, since route-finding in cloud/fog on the open ridge can be tricky.
Budget significant extra time for the up-and-down undulation of the ridge crest — GPS-tracked hikes commonly log more gain (3,300+ ft) than simple trailhead-to-summit elevation math would suggest.
watchOut: As of the current Forest Service alert, FS Road 37 is washed out at milepost 3.1 with no repair date, making the Skyline Divide Trailhead inaccessible by vehicle — check current Forest Service alerts before planning this approach.
Loose scree and talus on the final summit push; a short Class 3 step near the top has real exposure.
Snow can linger on the ridge into July, requiring an ice axe and self-arrest skill on the traverse.
knownHazards: Loose rock and talus on the final Class 2/3 scramble to the summit.
Residual snow on the ridge crest into early summer creates slip-and-fall hazard on steep side-hill terrain.
Route-finding difficulty in poor visibility, since the trail fades to an unmarked boot-path along much of Chowder Ridge.

climate.fall: Often clear and dry with fewer bugs, but shorter daylight and increasing chance of early-season snow by October.

routes.corrections: No specific 'Skyline Divide' route name is attested as an official climbing-route title distinct from the trail/ridge it follows; treated here as the standard descriptive name used by trip reports and FKT for the trailhead-to-summit line. Summit elevation is reported variably across sources (7,415-7,522 ft); 7,513 ft (PeakVisor/community consensus) was used here as the best-converged figure. Distance/gain figures (11.9 km one-way, 2,818 ft gain) come from FastestKnownTime's dedicated route page; other GPS-tracked trip reports report higher cumulative gain (up to ~3,300 ft) due to ridge undulation not captured in simple point-to-point stats.

routes.overview: A shorter, higher-starting alternative to the Skyline Divide approach, climbing the Cougar Divide ridge trail from the end of FS Road 33 to Chowder Ridge before joining the same Class 2 scramble to Hadley Peak's summit.
routes.beta: From the Cougar Divide Trailhead (~4,900 ft), the maintained Cougar Divide Trail #601 climbs along a ridge crest through meadows and old growth to its high point near 5,920 ft (about 3.1 miles in). From there the route continues cross-country along the open ridge, crossing toward Dobbs Cleaver/Chowder Ridge, to merge with the Skyline Divide approach below Hadley Peak's northwest ridge. The final push to the summit is Class 2 with a brief Class 3 step over loose scree, with generally mild exposure for experienced scramblers.
routes.approach: From Glacier, WA, drive Mt. Baker Highway (SR 542) east and turn onto Forest Service Road 33; follow roughly 11 miles of gravel road (rough in sections, high-clearance/SUV recommended, sedans passable with care) to its end at the Cougar Divide Trailhead/parking (room for 8-10 vehicles). Note the true trailhead may be roughly another kilometer past where online mapping apps route. Hike Cougar Divide Trail #601 along the ridge to its high point (~5,920 ft), then continue cross-country northeast along the open ridge, crossing near Dobbs Creek, to Chowder Ridge and the base of Hadley Peak's final scramble.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: downclimb the loose summit scramble, retrace the ridge back to the Cougar Divide Trail high point, and follow the maintained trail back to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the short Class 3 step and loose scree back onto the connecting ridge, then retrace the cross-country ridge traverse back to the Cougar Divide Trail's high point (~5,920 ft). Follow the maintained Cougar Divide Trail #601 back down through meadows and forest to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done car-to-car in a single day given the shorter, higher-starting approach: an early start, roughly 5-6 hours to the summit and back given the ~10.6-mile round trip and ~3,600 ft of cumulative gain.
routes.bail: The route can be abandoned at any point by retracing the ridge crest and Cougar Divide Trail back to the trailhead; no fixed rappel or technical retreat is required.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that allows a full daylight margin for the return along the ridge and loose summit scramble, and avoid the final scramble block in wet or icy conditions given the loose rock underfoot.
routes.bestSeason: The higher Cougar Divide trailhead (~4,900 ft, versus Skyline Divide's lower start) makes this a shorter outing once the seasonal road gate opens; ideal timing is roughly mid-August through early October, avoiding early-season mosquitoes and late-season snow on the ridge.
whatToBring: Recreation/day-use pass or annual Interagency pass for the trailhead
self-issue wilderness permit from the trailhead box
extra water/treatment
layers for exposed ridge walking
sun protection
insect repellent (mosquitoes common into midsummer)
routes.proNeeds: No fixed protection or rope is used on the standard scramble line; an ice axe is the main security item carried, useful for residual snow on the ridge in early or late season.
proTips: Cougar Divide's trailhead sits notably higher (~4,900 ft) than Skyline Divide's, making this the shorter and less-crowded of the two standard approaches.
FS Road 33 is gated seasonally (closed roughly Nov 1-Jul 7 for wildlife protection) — confirm the gate is open before driving out.
The actual trailhead is reported to be roughly a kilometer beyond where mapping apps typically route drivers along FS 33 — don't stop early.
watchOut: FS Road 33 is closed to vehicles annually from November 1 through July 7 to protect wildlife; the route is not accessible by this approach outside that window.
Loose rock and a steep dropoff to one side along parts of the ridge traverse.
Severe mosquitoes are commonly reported until mid-summer on the meadow sections.
knownHazards: Loose rock and talus on the final Class 2/3 scramble to the summit.
Steep, loose terrain along sections of the ridge traverse with a drop-off to one side.
Snow on the ridge can persist into early summer, requiring an ice axe and creating slip hazard on the traverse.

blurb: Helmet Butte is a 7,420-foot summit of granodiorite and quartz diorite on the Cascade Crest in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, straddling the Chelan/Snohomish county line about 8 miles east-northeast of Glacier Peak and immediately northeast of Buck Creek Pass. It was named by longtime Cascades surveyor and explorer Albert H. Sylvester, who considered the surrounding Buck Creek country among the most beautiful he had seen in the range. The peak's meadowy approach slopes and well-defined summit block make it a popular objective for backpackers and peakbaggers seeking a straightforward Class 3 scramble with big views of Fortress Mountain, Clark Mountain, and the Glacier Peak high country, without the commitment of the range's higher summits.
hierarchyNote: None - the peak checks out. Given coordinates (48.149525, -120.955365) closely match Wikipedia's published position (48°08'58"N 120°57'19"W ≈ 48.1494, -120.9553) and the described location (Glacier Peak Wilderness, Cascade Crest, immediately NE of Buck Creek Pass, ~8 mi ENE of Glacier Peak) is consistent across SummitPost, Wikipedia, Peakbagger and ListsOfJohn. One data-quality issue found: the existing elevationFt (7,372 ft) is inconsistent with sourced figures - see the route's corrections field.

objHaz: Loose rock on parts of the Class 3 summit block
Steep, unmaintained grass/heather slopes (west-side option) become slick and hazardous when wet
Braided, unsigned use-paths above ~5,800 ft require route-finding
Remote wilderness approach with no reliable cell coverage
routes.face: South side of the summit block (standard line); west side of the block via Buck Creek Pass (alternate)
routes.overview: A non-technical but exposed Class 3 scramble to a well-defined granodiorite summit block, reached via a long approach up the Buck Creek Pass Trail and then an off-trail push through meadows above Pass No Pass (or, alternately, up steep grass/heather from Buck Creek Pass).
routes.beta: Helmet Butte is normally climbed as part of a Buck Creek Pass backpacking trip rather than a car-to-car day climb, given the ~17.6-mile round-trip approach. From a point at 5,600 ft on the Buck Creek Pass Trail ('Waypoint 86' on SummitPost), the recommended line turns off-trail toward Pass No Pass on a well-defined but braiding boot path, gains a notch around 7,200 ft below the summit block, then finishes with a short, low-exposure Class 3 scramble on solid rock, aiming for the block's south (left) side for the easiest line. An alternate line continues past the trail junction to Buck Creek Pass itself, then climbs steep grass and heather slopes to the west side of the summit block before the same short scramble - SummitPost notes this option is likely hazardous when wet.
routes.approach: From Coles Corner on US-2 (about 20 miles east of Stevens Pass), take WA-207 north 4.4 miles, turn right onto Chiwawa Loop Road for 1.3 miles, then left onto Chiwawa River Road (Forest Road 6200) and follow it roughly 23 miles to its end at the Trinity / Buck Creek Pass Trailhead (about 2,772-2,800 ft). IMPORTANT: as of May 20, 2026, FR 6200 is closed to vehicle traffic beyond Atkinson Flats Campground, cutting off drive-in access to Trinity indefinitely - confirm current road status with the Chelan Ranger District before relying on this approach. From the trailhead, hike the Buck Creek Trail #1513 (signed Buck Creek Pass Trail #789 on Forest Service pages), climbing steadily through forest and along Buck Creek roughly 8.8 miles to about 5,600 ft, where the standard line breaks off-trail to the right toward Pass No Pass.
routes.descent: Descend the same line: reverse the Class 3 scramble off the south side of the summit block, then retrace the boot path back down to the Buck Creek Pass Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the short Class 3 block on its south side, descend the grassy slopes back through the Pass No Pass notch (or, if the west-side variant was used, back down the steep grass/heather to Buck Creek Pass), rejoin the maintained Buck Creek Pass Trail at about 5,600 ft, and follow it roughly 8.8 miles back down to the Trinity Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to Trinity Trailhead (road access currently closed - verify before departure) and backpack the ~8.8-mile approach to a camp at Pass No Pass or Buck Creek Pass, both of which have multiple established sites. Day 2: Day-scramble Helmet Butte from camp (SummitPost estimates 1-2 hours round trip from Pass No Pass); many parties combine the outing with nearby Fortress Mountain or Liberty Cap given the shared approach. Break camp and hike partway or fully out. Day 2 or 3: Finish hiking out to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward in either direction on the scramble portion - simply reverse whichever line was used back down to the Buck Creek Pass Trail junction at about 5,600 ft, then follow the maintained trail out to Trinity.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time that gets the party off the exposed summit block and open meadow slopes before weather deteriorates, and avoid the steep west-side grass/heather option entirely if it is wet, since traction there is poor.
routes.bestSeason: June through October offers the most reliable conditions per route reports, with the window potentially stretching from May into November depending on that year's snowpack. Early-season parties should expect lingering snow on the approach and the off-trail scramble slopes.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected in the Glacier Peak Wilderness backcountry around Buck Creek Pass and Pass No Pass; carry a satellite messenger or PLB for emergencies.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock or ice gear is needed - this is a hiking/scrambling route. An ice axe (and possibly microspikes) is worth carrying in the shoulder seasons for lingering snow on the approach and summit slopes.
routes.proNeeds: None - the Class 3 summit block is unroped scrambling on generally solid rock with a short, low-exposure crux.

routes.corrections: The existing database elevation of 7,372 ft appears low. SummitPost and ListsOfJohn both give 7,420 ft; Wikipedia rounds to 7,400 ft (2,256 m); Peakbagger's page title also references 7,420 ft in third-party citations. Recommend correcting elevationFt to 7,420 ft (retain a note that some sources round to 7,400 ft). Prominence is also cited inconsistently between SummitPost (1,040 ft) and Wikipedia (1,000 ft/305 m) - not corrected here since this schema has no prominence field, but flagged for awareness.

blurb: Gilbert Peak enriched with two sourced routes (Meade Glacier standard route via Conrad Meadows/Klickton Divide, and the West Route via Snowgrass Flats/Goat Ledges). Hierarchy check passed — no corrections needed to peak name, coordinates, elevation, or parent area.

routes.overview: The standard/easiest ascent: a long approach through Conrad Meadows and over Klickton Divide, gaining the Meade Glacier's permanent snowfields on the peak's southeast side and finishing with a short class 3 scramble to the summit block.
routes.beta: From the Conrad Meadows / South Fork Tieton Trailhead, follow Trail #1120 through Conrad Meadows and up toward Warm Lake, then traverse southwest to Klickton Divide and follow the ridge (mixing snow and rock, with Gilbert in view most of the way) toward the summit block. Alternatively, climbers can cross the east side of the Conrad Glacier to the saddle east of the summit, drop onto the Meade Glacier, and climb it (now largely reduced to persistent snowfields rather than a crevassed glacier) to a rock outcrop below the final pitch. Either way, the route tops out with a steep snow/scree slope and a short, loose class 3 scramble through a notch to the true summit. Very popular and rated the most straightforward line up the peak, though it is a long day (~20 miles round trip) with real elevation gain.
routes.approach: Drive to the Conrad Meadows / South Fork Tieton Trailhead (Trail #1120, elev. ~4,044 ft): from Naches, WA take US-12 W about 22 miles, turn onto Forest Road 1200 (Tieton Reservoir Road) for 4.5 miles, then onto Forest Road 1000 (South Fork Tieton Road) for 12.6 miles to the trailhead at road's end (last ~7 miles gravel, generally in good condition). Hike Trail #1120 through Conrad Meadows (crossing Conrad Creek) into the Goat Rocks Wilderness, continuing toward Surprise Lake and Warm Lake, then climb southwest to gain Klickton Divide near Point 6,882 before following the ridge northwest toward the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent route down the summit scramble, the snowfield, and Klickton Divide back to Conrad Meadows Trail #1120.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same class 3 notch and steep snow/scree slope used on ascent, then follow Klickton Divide back southeast. Rejoin the boot path down through Warm Lake basin and Conrad Meadows to the trailhead. Many parties vary the return via Surprise Lake for scenery; glissading the snowfield below the summit is common in early/mid season when snow is consolidated (self-arrest gear required).
routes.itinerary: Typically done as an overnight/2-day backpack: Day 1, hike in to a camp at Surprise Lake (wooded, sheltered) or Warm Lake (above treeline, exposed to wind); Day 2, alpine start for the summit push over Klickton Divide and the Meade Glacier snowfields, then return to camp and hike out (or split the hike-out over a 3rd day). Strong parties have done it car-to-car in a long single day.
routes.bail: The ridge/glacier approach has no major technical commitment below the final summit scramble — parties can retreat along Klickton Divide or back down the snowfield to Warm Lake/Conrad Meadows at essentially any point before the summit notch.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before starting the final snow/scree slope and summit scramble — afternoon thunderstorms and softening, rockfall-prone snow are the main reasons to abandon the summit push and retreat the way you came.
routes.bestSeason: Late June through October is climbable, but the route is very snow-dependent: early season (June-July) offers a firmer, more continuous snow climb up the Meade Glacier snowfields but requires crampons/ice axe and avalanche awareness; by late summer (Aug-Sept) the snow largely melts out, exposing a rougher boot path and looser rock on the final scramble; conditions vary significantly year to year with snowpack.
routes.detailedRack: Not a roped/protected climb for most parties; essential gear is glacier/snow travel kit rather than a rock rack.
whatToBring: Trekking poles
Sun protection
Layers for exposed ridge wind
Water treatment (creeks can run low/dry late season)
Overnight backpacking gear if camping at Surprise Lake or Warm Lake
proTips: Mark distinctive terrain features on the ascent (the route through meadows and snow patches is easy to lose on the return, especially in flat, featureless sections near Warm Lake).
An early-morning start firms up the snowfield for easier travel and reduces rockfall/wet-snow hazard on the final scramble.
Camping at Surprise Lake gives a more sheltered, wooded camp than the exposed, windy Warm Lake basin.
watchOut: Black bears and mountain goats are regularly encountered along Klickton Divide and near camp — store food properly.
The final class 3 scramble has real exposure despite its low technical grade; loose rock is common.
Downed trees and a lost/faint trail in the lower forest and around Warm Lake in early season can slow travel significantly.
knownHazards: Steep snowfield below the summit requires solid self-arrest skills.
Loose rock on the summit scramble creates rockfall risk for parties climbing close together.
The Conrad and Meade Glaciers are receding but can still harbor hidden crevasses/moats in early season.
Long, remote approach (~20 miles round trip) with limited water in late-season dry years.

pitchDetail.notes: Trail #1120 through Conrad Meadows to Surprise Lake / Warm Lake, ~8-9 mi one way.
Ridge travel mixing snow and loose rock, Gilbert visible most of the way.
Snow travel/glacier crossing on the southeast side, crampons recommended.
Short, loose scramble through a notch to the true summit; no significant exposure on most lines but rock is unstable.

road.name: Forest Road 1200 (Tieton Reservoir Rd) then Forest Road 1000 (South Fork Tieton Rd)
road.status: Paved to gravel; last ~7 miles gravel, generally good condition
road.driveNote: From Naches: US-12 W ~22 mi, then FR 1200 for 4.5 mi, then FR 1000 for 12.6 mi to the Conrad Meadows/South Fork Tieton Trailhead (#1120) at road's end.

climate.typical: High alpine volcanic terrain with persistent snowfields into midsummer; afternoon thunderstorms common July-August.
climate.spring: Heavy, unconsolidated snowpack; trail typically not melted out until late June/July.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep) but also most crowded and thunderstorm-prone.
climate.fall: Snow-free but shortening days and early-season snow possible by October.
climate.winter: Not a winter objective for this route; approach roads may be gated/impassable.

access.landManager: Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Naches Ranger District) for the Conrad Meadows approach; the summit itself sits within Goat Rocks Wilderness (jointly administered with Gifford Pinchot National Forest)
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent Interagency Pass may be required at trailhead parking
access.permit: Free, self-issued Goat Rocks Wilderness permit required at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass
access.closures: No camping within 100 ft of the Pacific Crest Trail or lakes; group size and campfire restrictions apply within the Wilderness.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn alpine start if attempting car-to-car, or early morning from Surprise Lake/Warm Lake camp

waypoints.name: Conrad Meadows / South Fork Tieton Trailhead (#1120)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Yakima County Sheriff's Office (509) 574-2500
emergency.rangerStation: Naches Ranger District, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest
emergency.nearestHospital: Astria Toppenish / Virginia Mason Memorial, Yakima, WA (nearest full-service hospital to the eastern approach)
emergency.notes: Cell coverage is unreliable to absent through most of the approach and on the peak; this is a remote, lightly-trafficked route relative to Mt. Rainier/Mt. Adams — expect slow rescue response times.

routes.corrections: Given peak elevation (8,184 ft) and coordinates match Wikipedia, WTA, and Peakbagger data closely; no correction needed. Note some sources cite slightly different historical elevations (8,201 ft on the 1904 USGS map; 8,204 ft on listsofjohn.com) reflecting older surveys, not a data error.

routes.overview: A longer, looser, more adventurous line up Gilbert Peak's west side from Snowgrass Flats, traversing the exposed 'Goat Ledges/Goat Ribs' and a band of solid white volcanic aggregate to a notch behind a dark rock tower ('the black prow') before a class 4 finish to the summit.
routes.beta: From a base camp at Snowgrass Flats, climbers cross south toward the Old Snowy/Ives Peak massif and traverse a series of rock ribs and shallow gullies (the 'Goat Ledges' and 'Goat Ribs') at roughly 7,400-7,600 ft toward Gilbert Peak. The key navigational feature is a band of solid, abrasive white pyroclastic aggregate that points toward a notch behind a prominent dark rock tower nicknamed the 'black prow'; staying on this band (rather than the looser rock above or below it) is described as the safest line. A lower variation holds a consistent ~7,500 ft traverse elevation to reduce exposure to party-induced rockfall. The route is regarded by some as loose and dangerous, but careful route-finding along the white band and goat paths allows a safer class 4 passage to the summit.
routes.approach: Drive to the Berry Patch or Snowgrass Flat Trailhead (Chambers Lake area) off Forest Road 21/2150 on the west side of the Goat Rocks: turn east onto FR 21, continue about 13 miles, turn at the Snowgrass Trail/Chambers Lake sign onto FR 2150, then follow signs to the Snowgrass Flat Trailhead (elev. ~4,650 ft). Hike Trail #96 (Snowgrass) roughly 4 miles and ~2,000 ft of gain along Goat Creek to Snowgrass Flats, a large subalpine meadow used as base camp; from there head cross-country/scramble east across the Goat Ledges/Goat Ribs traverse toward Gilbert Peak (often combined with Old Snowy Mountain and Ives Peak in one traverse day).
routes.descent: Reverse the Goat Ledges/Goat Ribs traverse and white-aggregate-band route back to Snowgrass Flats camp, then hike out Trail #96.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the class 4 summit section back through the notch behind the black prow, then carefully retrace the white aggregate band across the Goat Ribs/Goat Ledges at the same ~7,500 ft traverse elevation used on ascent to minimize rockfall exposure to teammates below. From Snowgrass Flats, descend Trail #96 back to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a multi-summit traverse from a Snowgrass Flats camp: Day 1, hike in to Snowgrass Flats (or Lower Snowgrass); Day 2, traverse to and climb Old Snowy Mountain, Ives Peak, and Gilbert Peak in a single long day (reported round-trip totals of ~22 miles and ~7,500 ft cumulative gain/loss for the full multi-peak traverse) before returning to camp; Day 3, hike out.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward along the same white-aggregate traverse band back toward Snowgrass Flats at any point before committing to the final class 4 notch; avoid descending off-route into the looser rock above or below the band.
routes.turnaround: Turn around before starting the exposed Goat Ribs traverse or the summit notch if weather is deteriorating, snow/ice coats the aggregate band, or the party is moving slower than planned — the loose rock becomes markedly more hazardous in wet or low-light conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Best from late July through September once seasonal creeks and snow patches on the traverse have mostly melted out; the Snowgrass/Old Snowy/Goat Rocks area is one of the most heavily used parts of the wilderness in peak summer season.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed protection or roped pitches reported; parties travel unroped over class 3-4 terrain.
whatToBring: Trekking poles
Sun protection (largely open, exposed terrain)
Extra water (creeks can run dry by late summer)
Backpacking/camping gear for a Snowgrass Flats base camp
proTips: Follow the white pyroclastic aggregate band specifically — it's described as solid and abrasive ('like a climbing wall') compared to the looser dark rock around it.
Hold a consistent traverse elevation (~7,500 ft) with your party to avoid knocking rock down onto teammates below.
Goat paths along the Goat Ribs/Goat Ledges are a useful route-finding aid.
watchOut: The route is explicitly described by experienced parties as loose and dangerous off the recommended line.
The 'black prow' rock tower and surrounding terrain are loose volcanic breccia — test holds before weighting them.
Wildfire smoke has reduced visibility on this traverse in past (Aug/Sept) seasons.
knownHazards: Significant rockfall potential on the Goat Ribs/Goat Ledges traverse, especially with parties stacked at different elevations.
Loose andesite/pyroclastic breccia and tuff throughout the upper mountain.
Steep, sketchy sections on the approach to the summit block reported even in late-season dry conditions.
Route-finding error risk — the correct line depends on identifying the white aggregate band correctly.

pitchDetail.notes: Trail #96, ~4 mi and ~2,000 ft gain to camp.
Cross-country traverse of ribs and gullies at ~7,400-7,600 ft toward Gilbert Peak.
Solid, abrasive volcanic aggregate leads to the notch behind the black prow.
Short class 4 scramble through the notch to the true summit; loose in places.

road.name: Forest Road 21, then Forest Road 2150 (Chambers Lake / Snowgrass Flat Rd)
road.status: Gravel forest road; typically open by mid-summer
road.driveNote: Turn east onto FR 21, continue ~13 mi to the Snowgrass Trail/Chambers Lake sign, then follow FR 2150 (and signed spurs) to the Snowgrass Flat/Berry Patch Trailhead.

climate.typical: Popular, heavily-used alpine terrain; open, exposed traverse with little shade.
climate.spring: Low-elevation trail sections clear by mid-May; high terrain typically not clear until mid-July.
climate.summer: Prime season but the most crowded part of the Goat Rocks Wilderness; afternoon storms possible.
climate.fall: Good conditions into September; running water sources can dry up.
climate.winter: Not applicable for this scrambling route.

access.landManager: Gifford Pinchot National Forest (Cowlitz Valley Ranger District)
access.fees: Valid Recreation Pass / Northwest Forest Pass required at the trailhead
access.permit: Free, self-issued Goat Rocks Wilderness permit required at the trailhead
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Interagency Pass
access.closures: No camping or campfires at Snowgrass Flats itself (camp at designated sites such as Lower Snowgrass or Bypass Camp instead); no camping within 100 ft of the PCT or lakes.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning start from Snowgrass Flats camp to allow a full day for the Old Snowy/Ives/Gilbert traverse

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Lewis County Sheriff's Office (360) 748-9286
emergency.rangerStation: Cowlitz Valley Ranger District, Gifford Pinchot National Forest, (360) 497-1100
emergency.nearestHospital: Arbor Health / Morton Hospital, 521 Adams Ave, Morton, WA (nearest hospital to the western/Packwood-Randle approach)
emergency.notes: This is one of the most heavily-visited corners of the Goat Rocks Wilderness in summer, but cell coverage on the traverse itself is limited; carry a satellite communicator for the exposed Goat Ribs section.

blurb: Wing Peak (5,761 ft) is a remote summit in the Skykomish Mountains of the Central Cascades, within the Wild Sky Wilderness (Mt. Baker–Snoqualmie National Forest, Snohomish County, WA). It sits on the same ridge system as Gunn Peak and Tailgunner Peak above the town of Baring along the US Highway 2 / Stevens Pass corridor, and is built from metamorphic rock of the Gunn Peak Formation (chert, quartzite, gneiss) intruded by Cenozoic granodiorite of the Index Batholith. No documented first ascent exists for Wing Peak itself; it is climbed almost exclusively as an extension of the well-known but demanding Gunn Peak–Tailgunner Peak scramble traverse, reached by a long, largely off-trail approach.

routes.rappels: 1-2 short rappels reported: off the summit and down the upper snow face on the northwest ridge; rope length/anchor details not documented beyond parties carrying a single 30m rope.
routes.overview: A remote scramble up Wing Peak's northwest ridge from Tailgunner Pass, mixing steep snow faces and rock benches with a final exposed snow/ice finger and short Class 4 summit block; almost always combined with neighboring Gunn Peak and Tailgunner Peak in the same trip.
routes.beta: Wing Peak has no maintained trail. Parties approach via the Barclay Creek/Tailgunner Creek drainage to Tailgunner Pass, then climb steep snow faces and rock benches along the peak's northwest ridge. The crux is a roughly 70-degree, heavily exposed snowfinger just below the summit, above which loose Class 4 rock leads to the top. Most parties climb Wing as part of a 2-3 day traverse with Gunn Peak and Tailgunner Peak, camping near Tailgunner Pass.
routes.approach: From the Barclay Lake Trailhead (~2,300 ft, reached via Forest Road 6024 off US Highway 2 in Baring), follow Barclay Creek Trail 1055, then leave the maintained trail to bushwhack along Barclay Creek and up Tailgunner Creek to an old, overgrown logging road. A bootpath climbs through forest to about 4,000 ft, crosses a Class 2-3 waterfall ravine, then follows the Tailgunner Couloir (grass and snow patches) to Tailgunner Pass (roughly 5,320-5,360 ft), which lies WNW of the Wing Peak summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the northwest ridge back to Tailgunner Pass, with 1-2 short rappels off the summit and upper snow face, then descend the Tailgunner Couloir and the bushwhack approach route back to the Barclay Lake Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb/rappel a short distance, then rappel the steepest section of the upper snow face. Below that, careful cramponing is needed through a rock defile where a narrow ribbon of snow winds between deep moats — described by one party as the most unnerving part of the descent. Continue down the northwest ridge's snow faces and benches to Tailgunner Pass, then down the Tailgunner Couloir and the bushwhack drainage back to Barclay Creek Trail and the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Commonly done as a 2-3 day trip: Day 1, hike/bushwhack from the Barclay Lake Trailhead to camp near Tailgunner Pass (~3,200-3,400 ft gain, roughly 6-7 hrs); Day 2 (optional), tag Gunn Peak and/or Tailgunner Peak from camp; final day, climb Wing Peak's northwest ridge from camp (~2.5 hrs up), then descend all the way to the trailhead (~4 hrs).
routes.bail: Retreat is by reversing the ascent; below the snowfinger, downclimbing/rappelling back to Tailgunner Pass is the only documented bail option, as no lower escape routes off the ridge have been reported.
routes.turnaround: Given the long, trail-less approach and the exposed, refreeze-dependent snowfinger crux, turn back before the snow softens in afternoon sun and before deteriorating weather removes the visibility needed for the unmarked bushwhack descent.
routes.bestSeason: Documented ascents cluster from June through August, when the Tailgunner Couloir and northwest ridge still hold enough consolidated snow for cramponing; by late summer the route reportedly sheds its snow cover and becomes looser rock scrambling with less protection.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is reported as unreliable in the Barclay/Tailgunner Creek drainage; carry an offline map/GPS track and consider a satellite communicator given the remote, off-trail nature of the route.
routes.detailedRack: Parties have climbed the route with a single 30m rope, one snow picket, and about six assorted slings for protecting the snowfinger crux and building rappel anchors off the summit and upper snow face; crampons and an ice axe are standard for the couloir and ridge snow.
routes.proNeeds: A light rack — a picket or two for the snowfinger crux and slings for rappel anchors (no fixed anchors reported) — plus crampons and an ice axe for the snow sections.
whatToBring: Offline map/GPS track (cell coverage unreliable)
Bushwhacking layers for brush on the lower approach
Overnight camping gear if combining with Gunn/Tailgunner Peaks
Northwest Forest Pass for trailhead parking
proTips: Climb in late spring/early summer while the Tailgunner Couloir and northwest ridge still hold consolidated snow — the route gets looser and less protectable once it melts out.
Camp near Tailgunner Pass to split up the long approach and pick off Gunn Peak and Tailgunner Peak in the same trip.
Bring a rope, a picket, and a few slings — parties have belayed/rappelled the snowfinger and summit rather than soloing them.
Download an offline GPS track before leaving the trailhead; there is no maintained trail above lower Barclay Creek and cell service is unreliable.
watchOut: The crux snowfinger below the summit is reported at roughly 70 degrees and severely exposed — a slip here would be consequential.
Loose Class 4 rock on the final summit block, especially hazardous when wet or dusted with snow.
A rock defile with deep moats below the summit snow face has been called the most unnerving part of the descent.

Barclay Creek Road (FR 6024) has a history of storm damage (mudslides, sagging roadbed, blowdown); confirm current access with the Skykomish Ranger District/Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving in.
knownHazards: Steep, potentially refrozen snow on the Tailgunner Couloir and northwest ridge requiring crampons and an ice axe.
An exposed roughly 70-degree snowfinger crux just below the summit.
Loose Class 4 rock on the summit block.
Long, unmaintained, brushy bushwhack approach with no trail above lower Barclay Creek.
Historic aircraft wreckage (fuselage panels, wing parts, propellers) litters the south side of the peak — a sharp-metal hazard; leave wreckage undisturbed.

pitchDetail.notes: Barclay Lake Trail to a Barclay/Tailgunner Creek bushwhack and an overgrown logging road up to about 4,000 ft.
Scramble crossing of a waterfall ravine around 4,000 ft.
Grass and snow patches leading to Tailgunner Pass (roughly 5,320-5,360 ft).
Series of steep snow faces and rock benches; crampons and running belays used on hard/refrozen snow.
Horribly exposed snowfinger below the summit horn; often protected with a picket and belayed or roped.
Final short rock section to the true summit; rappelled on descent.

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (Forest Road 6024)
road.status: Gravel forest road; has a documented history of storm damage (mudslides, sagging roadbed, blowdown) in recent years — verify current status via Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF road alerts before travel.
road.driveNote: From US Highway 2 at Baring (milepost 42), turn north onto FR 6024 opposite the Der Baring Store, cross the railroad tracks, and continue 4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead (about a 20-car lot).

climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate — Pacific frontal systems bring heavy precipitation to the west-facing Cascade slopes, especially as winter snow.
climate.spring: Wet, with lingering snowpack on north- and upper-mountain slopes into June; the standard route's snow sections depend on this snowpack.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Aug) and the most commonly climbed period; the route sheds snow cover as summer progresses.
climate.fall: Rain returns by September-October along with the first low-elevation snow at higher camps.

access.landManager: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Skykomish Ranger District (Wild Sky Wilderness)
access.fees: Day-use fee at the Barclay Lake Trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass or Interagency Pass honored.
access.permit: No wilderness quota/entry permit identified for Wild Sky Wilderness; a trailhead parking pass is required.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful/Interagency Pass)

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office / Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR): 425-388-3328 (non-emergency); dial 911 for emergencies.
emergency.rangerStation: Skykomish Ranger Station, 74920 NE Stevens Pass Hwy, Skykomish, WA 98288 (Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF)
emergency.nearestHospital: EvergreenHealth Monroe (formerly Valley General Hospital), 14701 179th Ave SE, Monroe, WA — serves the Skykomish Valley including Baring.
emergency.notes: Remote, off-trail terrain with unreliable cell coverage; Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue has conducted numerous missions in the Gunn/Tailgunner/Wing Peak group. Carry a satellite communicator and file a trip plan.

blurb: Big Snow Mountain (6,680 ft) is a remote summit in the Alpine Lakes Wilderness of the central Cascades, about 7 miles north of Snoqualmie Pass in eastern King County, Washington, rising between the Middle Fork Snoqualmie and Dingford Creek drainages and ringed by alpine lakes (Myrtle, Big Snow, Snowflake, Hardscrabble). The peak is built of Keechelus andesite with sound granitic rock on its steep East Buttress, considered some of the best granite climbing in the Alpine Lakes region. It was first climbed on July 20, 1917 by a party from The Mountaineers club; today it is known chiefly as a long, route-finding-intensive off-trail scramble, with a rarely repeated technical line up its east side.

routes.overview: The shorter and most commonly climbed line to the summit: an off-trail push from Myrtle Lake past Big Snow and Snowflake Lakes, up steep gullies/chutes to broad rock and snow slopes on the mountain's north side, finishing on class 2-3 terrain to the summit.
routes.beta: From the Dingford Creek Trailhead, follow the maintained Dingford Creek Trail past the Hester Lake Trail junction to Myrtle Lake, then leave the trail and work steep, brushy terrain up to Big Snow Lake. Traverse right around Snowflake Lake and ascend chutes/rock ribs onto the upper mountain's rock fields; the final approach to the summit is class 2-3 scrambling. Serious route-finding and bushwhacking are required, and failed attempts due to losing the line are common.
routes.approach: Drive to the Dingford Creek Trailhead gate via Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Rd / FSR 56 (about 18 mi from North Bend; the last several miles are rough gravel, high-clearance recommended). Hike the Dingford Creek Trail roughly 3.2 mi to the Hester Lake Trail junction, continue left (east) toward Myrtle Lake (about 2 more miles). From Myrtle Lake, leave the trail and climb steep, increasingly brushy terrain toward Big Snow Lake, then traverse right around Snowflake Lake before ascending gullies/a snow couloir on the mountain's NW flank to the upper rock fields.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line back down through the upper rock fields, around Snowflake and Big Snow Lakes, to Myrtle Lake and the Dingford Creek Trail.
routes.descentText: Downclimb the same chutes/couloir used on ascent, taking care on crumbly, mossy rock and any residual snow or ice, especially early in the season; traverse back around Snowflake Lake, drop to Big Snow Lake, then pick a line down through brush to Myrtle Lake and follow the Dingford Creek Trail out to the trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Doable as a long car-to-car day for fit, route-finding-savvy parties (a similar NW-slope trip report logged roughly 6.7 hrs up / 4.8 hrs down, about 11.5 hrs total). Many parties instead backpack in and camp at Myrtle Lake or Big Snow Lake to split the trip over two days.
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent gullies to Big Snow Lake and the maintained trail at Myrtle Lake; the crux of any retreat is downclimbing steep, loose, or snow-covered chutes above Big Snow/Snowflake Lakes in poor conditions.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time before setting out — this is a long, route-finding-intensive day with significant off-trail travel and exposure to afternoon heat and storms on open upper slopes; don't push for the summit if timing would put descent of the loose gullies into dusk.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed high summer into early fall (roughly June through September) once the approach gullies and Big Snow Lake basin have shed most snow. A snow couloir on the NW flank can hold snow into July, which some parties actually prefer for firmer, more secure travel over loose talus.
watchOut: Off-trail terrain between Myrtle Lake and Big Snow Lake is steep, brushy, and easy to lose the correct line on — many parties have turned back here
Rock in the summit gullies is often crumbly and mossy underfoot
Open granite slabs and talus are fully sun-exposed; summer heat has caused significant fatigue on this route
proTips: Carry a GPS track for the Myrtle Lake to Big Snow Lake off-trail section — the correct gully is easy to miss
Camping at Myrtle or Big Snow Lake breaks the long day into two more comfortable ones
Traverse right around Snowflake Lake to link into the correct summit gully
knownHazards: No maintained trail beyond Myrtle Lake; light foot traffic keeps any boot path faint
Steep gullies/chutes above Big Snow Lake can hold snow or ice well into summer
Remote, seldom-visited terrain with real self-rescue implications if injured
whatToBring: Topo map/GPS track — route-finding is the main difficulty
Extra water — long, exposed slog with little shade up high
Bug protection for the brushy lower approach

road.name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road (FSR 56) to Dingford Creek Trailhead
road.status: Rough native-surface forest road; high-clearance recommended for the last several miles. A washout was reported on FSR 56 at milepost 17.3 as of July 1, 2026 — check current USFS alerts before driving.
road.seasonalGate: Road is permanently gated at the Dingford Creek Trailhead (~18 mi from North Bend); no vehicle access beyond this point.
road.driveNote: From I-90 exit 34 in North Bend, follow Middle Fork Snoqualmie Rd about 12 mi to the Middle Fork Trailhead bridge, then continue on FSR 56 roughly 6 more miles of potholed gravel to the gate at Dingford Creek Trailhead.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascades climate — most precipitation falls October through April; summers are comparatively dry and mild.
climate.spring: Cool and wet with significant lingering snowpack in the approach gullies and lake basins into June.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep); still expect afternoon thunderstorm potential and full sun exposure on the upper slabs.
climate.fall: Can offer clear, stable stretches into September/October before winter storms return.
climate.winter: Heavy snow and avalanche hazard; the route is not a standard winter objective.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, Snoqualmie Ranger District, within the Alpine Lakes Wilderness
access.fees: Current USFS trailhead page lists no fee at Dingford Creek Trailhead; some trip reports note a Northwest Forest Pass has historically been requested for parking here.
access.permit: Free self-issue wilderness permit required at the trailhead to enter Alpine Lakes Wilderness.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass recommended (not listed as required by the current USFS trailhead page).
access.closures: Middle Fork Snoqualmie Rd/FSR 56 is gated at the Dingford Creek Trailhead; a road washout was reported at FSR 56 MP 17.3 as of July 1, 2026.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn/early morning given the long, route-finding-heavy day

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff non-emergency: (206) 296-3311; 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Snoqualmie Ranger District, North Bend Office — (425) 888-1421, 902 SE North Bend Way, North Bend, WA
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — (425) 831-2300
emergency.notes: Remote off-trail terrain with no cell coverage expected; a satellite communicator (e.g., InReach) is recommended.

routes.corrections: Given DB elevation (6,680 ft) and coordinates (47.5348, -121.3659) are corroborated by Wikipedia (47°32'06"N 121°21'58"W, 6,680 ft) and Peakbagger; no correction needed. WTA/Mountain Project list a slightly lower elevation (~6,576-6,657 ft), likely from an older/different DEM source — the 6,680 ft figure is better corroborated across sources and retained. Timing figures are drawn from one representative trip report on a similar NW-slope line and will vary by party and conditions.

routes.overview: The historically preferred line: approach past Hardscrabble Lakes to a col, drop onto open granite slabs on the north slope, then follow the east ridge to the summit. Considered easier route-finding than the Dingford/north-slope line, but now a much longer trip since the access road was gated at Dingford Creek.
routes.beta: From the gated trailhead, walk or bike the old Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road roughly 7 miles to the unmarked boot path for Hardscrabble Lakes (near the old Hardscrabble Horse Camp). Climb past lower and upper Hardscrabble Lakes, staying close to the inlet to avoid cliff bands on climber's left. From upper Hardscrabble Lake, climb a gully to a col, drop onto the open granite slab of the north slope, then follow the east ridge to the summit.
routes.approach: Start at the Dingford Creek Trailhead gate and walk (or mountain-bike) the closed Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road about 7 miles to the boot path for Hardscrabble Lakes, which starts roughly half a mile short of the old Hardscrabble Horse Camp, across the road from a large fallen log. Follow the climbers path east through forest to upper Hardscrabble Lake, staying near the inlet — straying left leads into cliff bands.
routes.descent: Reverse the east ridge/north-slope line back to the col above Hardscrabble Lakes and down the gully to the lakes, then the long road walk back to the Dingford Creek Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the east ridge onto the north-slope granite slab, climb back up to the col, and descend the gully to upper Hardscrabble Lake; follow the inlet-side climbers path back down to the lower lake and rejoin the old road for the multi-mile walk-out.
routes.itinerary: Given the roughly 25-mile round trip created by the Dingford Creek gate closure, this route is typically done as an overnight backpack with a camp at Hardscrabble Lakes; a single-day car-to-car push is possible only for very fit, fast parties, some of whom bike the road-walk sections.
routes.bail: Retreat from anywhere on the ridge/slope is a straightforward reversal down to the col and gully back to Hardscrabble Lakes; the long road walk itself is the main time cost of any bail.
routes.turnaround: Because the approach alone can consume most of a day, set a conservative turnaround based on remaining road-walk/bike time and daylight, not just summit conditions.
routes.bestSeason: Snow-free travel is most reliable June through September; the road walk itself is passable much of the year, but the upper gully and col can hold snow into early summer.

routes.overview: A 10-pitch granite line up the crest of Big Snow Mountain's East Buttress above Hardscrabble Lake, first climbed with aid at NCCS III, 5.7, A1 in 1972 and later freed at 5.10 by Pete Doorish. A rarely repeated, semi-obscure Cascades classic on notably sound rock.
routes.beta: From lower Hardscrabble Lake, traverse its left (north) side and scramble over two rock bands to reach the south side of the buttress at about 5,400 ft. Climb close to the crest of the buttress, working under overhangs, on generally solid granite for 10 pitches (originally 4 pitches of class 4 followed by class 5 climbing with occasional aid pitons) to the summit area.
routes.approach: Approach as for the East Ridge/Hardscrabble Route: walk/bike the gated Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road to the Hardscrabble Lakes boot path, then continue to lower Hardscrabble Lake. Traverse the lake's left (north) side and scramble two rock bands to reach the buttress's south side at roughly 5,400 ft.
routes.itinerary: A full climbing day: long approach via Hardscrabble Lakes followed by 10 pitches of climbing: plan for an early start and be prepared for a long descent/approach reversal.
routes.bail: Retreat down the buttress or off its south side back toward the two rock bands above lower Hardscrabble Lake; specific rappel/bail anchors are not documented in available sources.
routes.turnaround: Given the remote, seldom-repeated nature of this route, set a conservative turnaround with ample daylight margin for a long, deliberate retreat if needed.
routes.bestSeason: Needs dry rock; best from midsummer into early fall (roughly July-September) once the approach and lower rock bands are snow-free.
routes.fa: Don Williamson and Jeff Dial, May 11, 1972 (original aid line, NCCS III 5.7 A1); later freed at 5.10 by Pete Doorish (free-ascent year not documented in available sources).
objHaz: Loose rock bands below the buttress base
watchOut: Two rock bands must be scrambled to reach the buttress base — exposed, loose terrain
Sustained face/crack climbing typical of an obscure 1970s Cascades first ascent; expect sparse fixed protection
Remote position — an injury here means a long, difficult self-rescue
proTips: There is no modern topo; bring the original AAC route description/beta and expect route-finding on the climb itself
Approach via Hardscrabble Lakes (not the Dingford/Myrtle Lake side) to reach the buttress base
The line has reportedly gone free at 5.10 — a light rack may suffice for a free ascent versus the original aided line
knownHazards: The east face and buttress drop directly into lower Hardscrabble Lake in steep walls
Route sees very few repeat ascents, so beta and any fixed anchors may be sparse or unreliable
Original ascent required aid pitons on Class 5 sections; free-climbing beta is limited to secondhand reports

pitchDetail.notes: Lower buttress, scrambling terrain on solid granite.

routes.proNeeds: The 1972 first ascent used occasional aid pitons on the Class 5 sections; a standard trad rack should suffice for parties repeating the route free, per the later 5.10 free ascent.

routes.overview: The standard route on Le Conte Mountain, an off-trail glacier-and-scramble line reached via the Ptarmigan Traverse: climbers approach from a camp at Yang Yang Lakes, skirt Peak 7004 on its east-slope talus/snow, and finish up an exposed, loose-rock ledge system to the summit.
routes.beta: From a base camp between the two Yang Yang Lakes, the route heads south around a forested ridge and ascends talus to a distinctive 'secret stair' — a precipitous path up compacted dirt and rock through a small gully to a grassy ledge. From there it follows the ridgeline to a rock outcropping beneath Le Conte's North Face, then works around (avoiding) Peak 7004 via east-slope talus and snow before an exposed traverse of narrow, loose ledges gains the summit. Rated class 4 scrambling; a rope, ice axe, and crampons are standard given the glacier travel required just to reach the peak.
routes.approach: Drive Cascade River Road from Marblemount (paved for roughly the first third, then gravel, narrow and steep near the end) 23.1 miles to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (road-end elevation about 3,660 ft). Hike the Cascade Pass Trail 3.7 miles / 1,700 ft gain to Cascade Pass, then continue cross-country/on climbers' path north on the Ptarmigan Traverse: ascend the Cache Glacier to Cache Col (~6,900 ft), descend to Kool-Aid Lake, cross the Middle Cascade Glacier, and continue to Yang Yang Lakes, which serves as the standard base camp below Le Conte's north side. This approach alone is a full day of glacier travel with sustained elevation gain and loss.
routes.descent: Reverse the summit ledges and east-slope talus/snow back to Yang Yang Lakes camp, then retrace the Ptarmigan Traverse approach back over the Middle Cascade Glacier, Kool-Aid Lake, and Cache Col/Cache Glacier to Cascade Pass and out the trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the exposed ledge traverse and loose rock back to the rock outcropping below the North Face, then descend the east-slope talus and snow to bypass Peak 7004, returning to the Yang Yang Lakes camp. From camp, retrace the Ptarmigan Traverse route: recross the Middle Cascade Glacier, climb back up to Kool-Aid Lake, ascend to Cache Col, and descend the Cache Glacier to Cascade Pass, then hike the maintained Cascade Pass Trail back to the trailhead. Parties commonly camp at Yang Yang Lakes or Pelton Basin/Cascade Pass on the way out rather than exiting in a single push.
routes.itinerary: Typical 2-3 day plan: Day 1 — drive to Cascade Pass Trailhead, hike to Cascade Pass and over Cache Col/Cache Glacier, descend to Kool-Aid Lake, cross Middle Cascade Glacier to Yang Yang Lakes camp (a long day, ~9,000+ ft cumulative gain over ~13 miles). Day 2 — climb Le Conte Mountain round-trip from camp via the east-slope/ledge route. Day 3 — reverse the approach back to the trailhead.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward but long: descend the same east-slope talus/snow and ledges back to Yang Yang Lakes, then reverse the glacier route over Kool-Aid Lake and Cache Col to Cascade Pass. There is no shorter bailout — any retreat from the peak or the traverse funnels back through Cache Col to the Cascade Pass Trail.
routes.turnaround: Because the route involves multiple glacier crossings and exposed loose-rock ledges near the summit, set a firm turnaround time based on remaining daylight and weather rather than pushing to complete the traverse — deteriorating visibility on the Cache/Middle Cascade Glaciers or wet rock on the summit ledges is reason enough to retreat to camp.
routes.bestSeason: Mid-July through mid-September is the standard window, once Cascade River Road has opened (typically late June, sometimes not until early July in heavy snow years) and glacier travel has consolidated; snow bridges and moat conditions on the Cache and Middle Cascade Glaciers change through the season, so early- and late-season parties should expect firmer glacier travel and possibly more crevasse exposure.
routes.comms: No reliable cell coverage should be expected anywhere along the Ptarmigan Traverse or on Le Conte Mountain itself; this is remote wilderness terrain and a satellite communicator or PLB is strongly recommended.
routes.fa: Calder T. Bressler, Ralph W. Clough, Bill Cox, Tom Myers — July 23, 1938 (peak first ascent; subsequent ridge ascents in 1953 and 1957)
objHaz: steep snow/ice sections below Red Ledge on approach
routes.detailedRack: Standard glacier-travel kit is required for the approach (rope, harness, crevasse-rescue gear, glacier axe, crampons); the class-4 summit ledges are typically soloed or protected minimally with a short rope for confidence on exposed steps.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection is used on the class 4 summit terrain; the technical requirement is glacier travel gear (axe, crampons, rope, crevasse rescue rack) for the approach rather than rock protection.

The 'secret stair' gully is the key to unlocking the ridge from the lakes — look for the compacted dirt/rock passage rather than forcing a line up loose talus.
Avoiding Peak 7004 via the east-slope talus and snow is faster and safer than a direct ridge traverse.
watchOut: Exposed summit ledges with loose rock and narrow footing near the top
Steep ice sections reported below 'Red Ledge' on the approach in some years
Crevasse hazard crossing the Cache and Middle Cascade Glaciers

knownHazards: Glacier crevasse hazard on the Cache Glacier and Middle Cascade Glacier sections of the approach.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Skagit County / USFS Road 15)

road.driveNote: 23.1 miles from State Route 20 at Marblemount to the Cascade Pass Trailhead (road-end elevation ~3,660 ft), about a one-hour drive.

climate.forecastZone: North Cascades / Washington Pass East Slopes zone (NWAC/NWS Mountain West zone)
climate.typical: Marine west coast climate with heavy winter precipitation, especially on west-facing slopes, from orographic lift off Pacific storm systems; summers are drier with a short weather-window climbing season.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; road and trail access to Cascade Pass frequently not open until June.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (Jul-Sep); glaciers most consolidated and crevasses more visible, though still present.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows into September/October; early snow can close out the season on the glaciated approach.
climate.winter: Heavy snow accumulation, road closed, route effectively inaccessible without full winter mountaineering approach.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Service Complex (approach via Cascade Pass Trail/Cascade River Road) and Glacier Peak Wilderness, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie / Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forests (peak itself straddles the wilderness boundary)
