routes.corrections: One trip-report aggregator (onehikeaweek.com, via a 'WA Top 300' style stat block) lists the summit elevation as 7,821 ft; this conflicts with Wikipedia, SummitPost/Beckey's Cascade Alpine Guide, and ListsOfJohn, which all independently confirm 7,762 ft (matching the given database value). Treated 7,821 ft as an error in that single weaker source; no change made to the existing 7,762 ft elevation. Also note ListsOfJohn lists prominence as 862 ft while Peakbagger's search snippet (page not directly fetchable) suggested 842 ft — could not fully cross-verify prominence; not included as a route field regardless.

blurb: Swiss Peak (7,988 ft) is a remote summit in the Northern Pickets, the northern arc of the Picket Range within North Cascades National Park's Stephen Mather Wilderness, sitting on the high alpine crest between West Fury and Spectre Plateau/Phantom Peak. Like most Northern Pickets summits it was first explored by Fred Beckey and partners beginning in the 1940s, and it is climbed almost exclusively by parties already committed to a multi-day Picket Range traverse rather than as a standalone objective. The summit block offers unusually solid rock for the range — a welcome contrast to the notoriously loose, chossy terrain on neighboring peaks — reached over glaciated approach terrain from either the West Fury col or the Pickell Pass/Spectre Plateau side.
hierarchyNote: Confirmed: Swiss Peak is a real, well-documented 7,988 ft summit in the Northern Pickets (Picket Range), North Cascades National Park, Whatcom County, WA — elevation matches exactly across Steph Abegg's trip report and peakery. Its parent placement under wa_picket_range is geographically correct. Note there is a separate, unrelated "Swiss Peak" (10,384 ft) in the Selkirk Mountains, Columbia-Shuswap district, British Columbia — do not conflate the two; several search/database hits (including a Peakbagger pid) point to the BC peak instead of this one. No independent lat/lng could be confirmed for the WA summit itself from a reputable source (Peakbagger blocked automated access and no GNIS coordinate was retrievable), so peak-level coordinates remain unset; this should be filled from a topo/GPX source later rather than guessed.

routes.overview: Swiss Peak has no independently approached line — it is climbed as a link in Picket Range crest traverses. The most-documented ascent gains the West Fury–Swiss col over a small glacier/snowfield, then climbs unusually solid rock up Swiss's southwest face to the broad NW–SE summit ridge; an alternate class 3 line ascends the south side directly from Pickell Pass, and parties approaching from the north describe an 'easy way up' from the Spectre Plateau.
routes.beta: Most ascents happen mid-traverse on the Northern Pickets high route (Luna–East Fury–West Fury–Swiss–Spectre Plateau–Phantom–Ghost–Challenger), first strung together as a continuous crest traverse by Wayne Wallace and Josh Kaplan in 2005 (VI 5.7 overall for the traverse, though Swiss itself is comparatively easy 3rd/4th-class terrain). From a camp near West Fury, cross a short glacier/permanent snowfield northwest of West Fury to the Swiss–Fury saddle, then climb the southwest face on notably solid rock (a welcome break from the loose ground nearby) to the summit ridge, descending near the northwest ridge to continue toward Spectre Plateau/Phantom Peak. A separate party approaching from the south (as part of a Mystery Ridge/Northern Pickets enchainment) climbed Swiss's south side directly from a camp at Pickell Pass: straightforward class 3 rock with some ridge-walking needed to identify the true high point, about 2.5 hours round trip. A reliable spring/running water source below the southwest snowfield is a notable resource on an otherwise dry crest.
routes.approach: Drive WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134). Descend about 1 mile on the Ross Dam trail/service road to Ross Lake, then cross via the Ross Lake Resort water taxi (on-demand, ~$4-5/person) or by hiking/paddling around, and pick up the Big Beaver Trail north along the lake. Follow it roughly 11 miles to Luna Camp, then continue about 1.5 miles further to the Access Creek drainage; leave the trail and climb the brushy, moderately steep Access Creek valley (staying on its north side is recommended) to the open basin east of Luna Peak (~4,300 ft) — described as the easiest, least-brushy approach on the east side of the Pickets. From there, gain Luna Col/Access Creek Col and traverse cross-country and glaciated terrain over Luna Peak, East Fury and West Fury to reach the Swiss–Fury saddle. An alternate southern approach reaches Swiss via Bacon Creek Road, Porkbelly Ridge, Mystery Ridge, Jasper Pass, Pioneer Ridge and Mt. Crowder's NE ridge down to a camp at Pickell Pass, from which Swiss's south side is climbed directly.
routes.descent: Reverse the southwest-face line back to the West Fury col, or continue the ridge crest north toward Spectre Plateau/Phantom Peak if still traversing; parties who came from the south descend back to Pickell Pass camp.
routes.descentText: There is no independent 'summit and go home' descent — retreat means reversing whichever approach line was used. From the summit, downclimb near the northwest ridge back toward the saddle with West Fury, re-cross the glacier/snowfield, and either return to a West Fury camp or continue the high traverse. Parties on the southern line downclimb the class 3 south side directly back to the Pickell Pass heather bench camp. Either way, exiting the range afterward requires committing to one of the long approach drainages (Access Creek to Big Beaver Trail/Ross Lake in the north, or Easy Ridge/Chilliwack/Hannegan Pass and Bacon Creek in the south) — both are multi-mile, partly off-trail hikes back to a trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Representative Northern Pickets crest plan (per published trip accounts): Day 1 — Ross Dam TH, water taxi across Ross Lake, Big Beaver Trail to Luna Camp/Access Creek. Day 2 — climb Access Creek drainage to the Luna Cirque basin, gain Luna Col, camp near Luna Col or continue toward East/West Fury. Day 3 — traverse East Fury, West Fury, cross the glacier/snowfield to the Swiss-Fury saddle, climb Swiss Peak's southwest face, continue toward Spectre Plateau. Day 4-5 — continue over Phantom Peak, Crooked Thumb, to Mt. Challenger and Whatcom Pass, exiting via Chilliwack River or Little Beaver trails. (A southern variant instead approaches via Bacon Creek/Porkbelly/Mystery Ridge over 4-5 days to Pickell Pass, climbs Swiss's south side in a 2.5-hour round trip from camp, then continues north over Challenger toward Whatcom Pass and out via Hannegan Pass.)
routes.bail: There is no quick low-elevation bailout from Swiss Peak itself — it sits deep in the range between long, arduous approach drainages. Retreat means backtracking the approach line already used: down Access Creek to the Big Beaver Trail and Ross Lake in the north, or back over Pioneer/Mystery Ridge toward Bacon Creek Road in the south. Deteriorating weather is best managed by turning back before committing to the glacier/snowfield crossing at the West Fury col.
routes.turnaround: Because retreat from anywhere on the Northern Pickets crest requires re-crossing glaciated and exposed ridge terrain, set a hard turnaround based on remaining daylight and weather trend well before reaching the summit — do not press on into afternoon whiteout or thunderstorm risk on this exposed, routefinding-intensive crest.
routes.bestSeason: Late July through September, once seasonal snow on the Access Creek approach and the Luna Cirque/West Fury glacier crossings has consolidated or melted back; both documented ascents occurred in the first half of August. Early-season attempts carry firmer, more continuous snow on the approach glaciers but greater avalanche and moat hazard; late-season visits trade easier snow travel for looser exposed rock and the onset of fall weather systems.
routes.comms: Cell coverage is unreliable to nonexistent through the North Cascades National Park backcountry, including the entire Picket Range interior; carry a satellite communicator (e.g. inReach/SPOT) for this multi-day, off-trail approach.
routes.detailedRack: A light glacier/scramble rack is typical rather than a full rock rack: a 30-50m rope for the glacier crossing and any short technical sections, a small rack of cams and nuts for protecting occasional low 5th-class moves on the traverse's harder sections, plus standard glacier travel gear (harness, crevasse-rescue kit, pickets) for the snowfield crossing to the West Fury col.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection needed on Swiss itself given its comparatively solid, moderate-angle rock; gear is carried primarily for the connecting glacier crossings and any exposed 4th/low-5th sections elsewhere on the traverse rather than for Swiss's summit block specifically.
whatToBring: multi-day backpacking/bivy gear
water treatment
satellite communicator (inReach/SPOT)
sturdy mountain boots
sun protection
bear canister (required for overnight backcountry camping in North Cascades NP)
proTips: The southwest snowfield below Swiss's summit block hides a running-water spring — a rare reliable water source on an otherwise dry section of the Northern Pickets crest.
From the south (Pickell Pass) side, don't stop at the first high point on the summit ridge — traverse to confirm the true summit, as the ridge has multiple bumps.
Parties approaching from Spectre Plateau to the north report finding 'the easy way up' Swiss from that side, suggesting some route-finding flexibility exists on the peak's north aspect.
watchOut: No maintained trail anywhere near the peak; all approach and connecting terrain is off-trail cross-country and glacier travel demanding solid navigation.
Neighboring peaks (Fury, Phantom) have notably looser rock than Swiss itself — don't assume Swiss's solid rock extends to the connecting ridge terrain.
Weather on this exposed crest can deteriorate quickly, as documented in multiple trip accounts; the remoteness makes weather-driven retreat slow and serious.
knownHazards: Small glacier/permanent snowfield crossing northwest of West Fury en route to the Swiss-Fury saddle carries crevasse and moat hazard.
Extreme remoteness — any injury or major weather event requires multi-day self-rescue; this is not a location with quick helicopter-accessible bailout.
Approach drainages (Access Creek, Porkbelly Ridge, Mystery Ridge) involve steep, brushy, sometimes cliffy off-trail travel with route-finding hazard independent of the peak itself.

pitchDetail.notes: Cross the small glacier/permanent snowfield northwest of West Fury to reach the Swiss-Fury col; crampons/axe useful depending on season.
Notably solid rock compared to neighboring summits; ascend to the broad NW-SE summit ridge, then walk the ridge to the true high point.
Straightforward scramble described as uncomplicated; ~2.5 hr round trip from a Pickell Pass camp; traverse the summit ridge to confirm the true summit.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA State Route 20)
road.status: Seasonally open; closed to through traffic in winter due to snow and avalanche hazard
road.seasonalGate: Typical winter closure gates near Ross Dam (MP 134) and Silver Star (MP 171), sometimes retreating further to Newhalem (MP 120)/Early Winters (MP 178) with heavy snow; closes roughly early December, reopens in spring (exact dates vary yearly with snowpack and any road repair work).
road.driveNote: Drive SR 20 to the Ross Dam Trailhead at milepost 134 (14 miles east of Newhalem); park at the trailhead and descend about 1 mile to Ross Lake to begin the approach.

climate.typical: Wet, maritime Cascade alpine climate with a short summer window; heavy winter snowpack lingers into July at this elevation on north-facing and glaciated terrain.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack; approach drainages and glaciers are heavily snow-covered, road access to Ross Dam TH may still be gated.
climate.summer: Primary climbing season (roughly late July-September); snow bridges and glacier crossings are more consolidated by mid-to-late summer, though afternoon thunderstorms occur.
climate.fall: Shortening weather windows and increasing storm frequency by late September/October; freeze-thaw makes rock and snow travel more variable.
climate.winter: Not a winter climbing objective; SR 20 access is gated and the range is subject to heavy, unstable snowpack and avalanche hazard.

access.landManager: National Park Service — North Cascades National Park, within the Stephen Mather Wilderness
access.fees: No park entrance fee; backcountry/wilderness camping permit required for all overnight stays (free, but a reservation fee applies for reserved permits via Recreation.gov).
access.permit: Wilderness/backcountry permit required year-round for overnight stays anywhere on the approach or in the range; obtained at the Wilderness Information Center in Marblemount (walk-up or Recreation.gov reservation).
access.closures: Placing new fixed anchors/bolts is prohibited under the park's wilderness climbing moratorium (clean-climbing techniques required); SR 20 access road is closed seasonally each winter.

timing.recommendedStart: Pre-dawn departure from high camp (West Fury col or Pickell Pass) to cross snow and glacier terrain while it is still firm.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Whatcom County Sheriff's Office non-emergency line: 360-676-6911 (call 911 for active emergencies)
emergency.rangerStation: North Cascades National Park Wilderness Information Center, Marblemount, WA — 360-854-7245; Park HQ (Sedro-Woolley) 360-854-7200
emergency.nearestHospital: Skagit Valley Hospital, Mount Vernon, WA (Level III trauma center); United General/PeaceHealth clinic in Sedro-Woolley is closer but smaller
emergency.notes: Search and rescue for this remote, off-trail, multi-day terrain is coordinated by North Cascades National Park climbing rangers; cell coverage is unreliable to absent throughout the approach and range, so carry a satellite communicator and file a trip plan/emergency contact with the Wilderness Information Center when picking up your permit.

routes.face: Southwest Face (primary); South Side from Pickell Pass (alternate)
routes.corrections: Given elevation (7,988 ft) matches sourced data exactly (Steph Abegg trip report, peakery) — no correction needed. Be aware a same-named but unrelated 10,384 ft 'Swiss Peak' exists in the Selkirk Mountains, BC (Peakbagger pid 2983); several search results and at least one Peakbagger listing surface that peak instead of this one and must not be conflated with wa_swiss_peak.

blurb: Fortress Mountain is an 8,679–8,684-foot peak in the Glacier Peak Wilderness of the North Cascades, the highest point on Chiwawa Ridge in the Chiwawa/Entiat Mountains group roughly 15 miles west of Lake Chelan, straddling the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie and Wenatchee National Forests. It's a broad, three-faced peak of notoriously loose, rotten rock, popular as a Bulger List (Washington's 100 highest peaks) objective and very commonly climbed together with neighboring Chiwawa Mountain from a shared Buck Creek Pass approach. Fred Beckey's research found the peak observed as early as 1911 and described then as "unscalable from any angle," but its actual first-ascent party and date were never documented.

routes.overview: The standard route: a long trail approach over Buck Creek Pass and Pass No Pass, followed by an unroped scramble up grass, scree, and blocky class 2-3 rock on Fortress's broad southwest face / south ridge to the summit.
routes.beta: Also referred to as the "Buck Creek route" or simply the standard/south ridge route. From the alpine basin below Pass No Pass, ascend green slopes and scree that steepen into class 2-3 talus and slab; the direct gully up the face is the loosest ground on the mountain, and many parties angle right onto slabbier rock or follow the south ridge crest instead. Reported ascent time from a high camp in the Pass No Pass basin is roughly 3.5-4 hours, with about 2.5-3 hours back down.
routes.approach: From Trinity Trailhead (2,800 ft) at the end of Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200, follow the Buck Creek Trail (#1513) roughly 9-9.5 miles, climbing through forest, an old avalanche debris field (~4,500 ft), and past an unmarked junction with the Pass No Pass way-trail (bears right, ~5,800 ft). Continue up the way-trail to Pass No Pass (~6,400 ft) and the alpine camping benches beyond, from which Fortress's southwest face and south ridge come into view. Note: since 2026, storm damage has closed FR 6200 to vehicles beyond Atkinson Flat Campground (about mile 16), adding roughly 7 miles of road-walking each way before even reaching the trailhead.
routes.descent: Descend the same southwest face/south ridge line; some parties prefer a cairned class 3 line on blocky terrain to skier's right of the ascent gully over down-climbing the loose gully directly.
routes.descentText: From the summit, retrace the ascent line down the south ridge / upper southwest face. The direct gully is the loosest ground on the mountain; a 2015 party found and cairned an alternate class 3 line over more solid, blocky terrain to skier's right of the gully that later parties have followed. Continue down through scree and talus to the grassy benches above Pass No Pass, back over the pass, and down the Buck Creek Trail to Trinity Trailhead.
routes.bail: Below the ridge/face, retreat is simply back down the ascent slopes to the Pass No Pass basin -- no technical downclimbing is required to bail beyond reversing the loose gully or its class 3 alternative. Above treeline the whole descent is exposed to weather with no faster escape than walking back out the Buck Creek Trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before committing to the loose southwest face gully -- the rock here is rotten and markedly worse to descend wet or in fading light. Cascades thunderstorms build most summer afternoons, so plan to be off the upper mountain before they arrive.
routes.bestSeason: Climbable roughly June through October depending on snowpack, with the best conditions July through September once the southwest face has mostly melted out and the Chiwawa River Road has opened for the season. An ice axe (and sometimes crampons) is recommended for early-season snow on the approach slopes below Pass No Pass.
routes.itinerary: Most parties make this a 2-3 day backpack: Day 1, hike in ~9-9.5 miles to camp at Buck Creek Pass or the alpine basin below Pass No Pass; Day 2, climb Fortress (very often combined with Buck Mountain and/or Chiwawa Mountain) from camp; Day 3, hike out. Fast parties have done it car-to-car in a single long day, with reported times ranging from under 12 hours up to about 19 hours when combined with other summits.
routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard class 2-3 line; a light rope, a few slings, and a couple of small cams/nuts are worth carrying for an optional hand-line on the loose gully, but most parties travel unroped.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal protection needs -- this is an unroped scramble for competent parties in summer conditions. Carry an ice axe (and crampons early season) for the snow sections below Pass No Pass; helmets are essential given constant rockfall potential.
gear: Light rope + a few slings (optional, for the loose gully)
whatToBring: Map/compass or GPS (the way-trail is faint and easy to lose under early-season snow)
Water treatment
Extra layers for the exposed ridge and summit block
Bear-aware food storage (Glacier Peak Wilderness)
proTips: The direct southwest face gully holds the loosest rock on the route; several parties recommend angling right onto slabbier class 3 rock or following the south ridge crest instead.
A cairned class 3 line over blocky terrain to skier's right of the ascent gully (found by a 2015 party) avoids some of the worst loose rock on descent.
Space climbers out (one party used a 20-minute gap) when ascending or descending the gully to reduce rockfall risk to those below.
Wear real boots, not trail runners -- multiple trip reports note the scree and wet grass chew through soft footwear.
Fortress is very commonly combined with Buck Mountain and/or Chiwawa Mountain from the same Buck Creek Pass / Pass No Pass camp.
watchOut: Notoriously loose, rotten rock throughout the upper mountain -- test every hold.
Persistent snow patches below the summit block can linger into midsummer, sometimes requiring an ice axe even in July/August.
Afternoon thunderstorms build quickly over the Glacier Peak Wilderness in summer.
The way-trail past Buck Creek Pass to Pass No Pass is rough and unmaintained -- easy to lose under early-season snow.
knownHazards: Rockfall from loose talus and scree on the southwest face gully, especially with other climbers above.
Steep snow slopes on the approach into early summer requiring ice axe self-arrest skills.
River/creek crossings and brushy, sometimes-indistinct trail sections lower on the approach.
A long additional road-walk (~7 miles each way) to reach Trinity Trailhead due to a 2026 storm-damage closure of FR 6200 beyond Atkinson Flat Campground.

road.name: Chiwawa River Road / FR 6200
road.status: Open to Atkinson Flat Campground (~mile 16); closed to vehicles beyond that point to Trinity Trailhead since 2026 due to storm damage
road.seasonalGate: Normally opens as snow clears, typically by June in an average year, once the current storm-damage closure is lifted; the final 12 miles are rough, native-surface road unsuitable for low-clearance vehicles even when open
road.driveNote: From Coles Corner on US-2, take WA-207 north ~4.2-4.4 mi, then Chiwawa Loop Road, then Chiwawa River Road/FR 6200 for ~23 mi to Trinity Trailhead (pavement ends around mile 11). Under the current closure, plan to park near Atkinson Flat and walk the final ~7 miles of road each way.

climate.typical: Wet, changeable North Cascades weather; orographic lift produces heavy precipitation, with the most reliable climbing weather July through September.
climate.spring: Deep, often unstable snowpack on approach slopes into June; river fords run high and cold with snowmelt.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jul-Sep); afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Glacier Peak Wilderness.
climate.fall: Good scrambling conditions into early-to-mid October in dry years, with shorter daylight.
climate.winter: Road access is snowed in and the peak sees only rare winter ascents over deep avalanche terrain.

routes.corrections: Sources disagree on Fortress's exact elevation: Wikipedia/USGS-derived figures list 8,679 ft, listsofjohn.com's LIDAR-based figure (8,684 ft) matches the existing database value and was retained, while several guidebook/trip-report sources (SummitPost, Lemke Climbs, Country Highpoints) cite an older 8,760 ft figure and many trip reports round to 8,674 ft.

routes.overview: A cross-country approach via the Chiwawa River drainage to the col between Chiwawa Mountain and Fortress, then a loose gully gaining Fortress's East Ridge, followed by better-quality ridge scrambling to an exposed 4th-class step just below the summit.
routes.beta: Also described by SummitPost as the "Chiwawa River route," this line is frequently combined with an ascent of neighboring Chiwawa Mountain from the same high camp at the ~7,200 ft col. Rock quality is notably better on the East Ridge itself than in the approach gully or on Fortress's southwest face.
routes.approach: From Trinity Trailhead, hike the Chiwawa River Trail (#1550) north along the valley, passing beneath Red Mountain, to around 6,580 ft, then leave the trail to traverse diagonally up toward Chiwawa Mountain's east ridge, descending to the roughly 7,200 ft col between Chiwawa and Fortress (or continuing over Chiwawa's summit first). Some parties instead approach via the Red Mountain Trail and Chiwawa Basin, fording the Chiwawa River. From the col, a loose class 3-4 gully gains the crest of Fortress's East Ridge.
routes.descent: Reverse the East Ridge and gully back to the Chiwawa-Fortress col, then descend to the bench and back down to the Chiwawa River/Buck Creek Trail to Trinity Trailhead.
routes.descentText: From the summit, down-climb the exposed 4th-class step and follow the East Ridge crest back to the head of the loose gully, descending it carefully to the ~7,200 ft col. From the col, either continue on to Chiwawa Mountain (a common combined objective) or drop back to the ~6,580 ft bench and retrace the cross-country line down to the Chiwawa River Trail, then out via the Buck Creek Trail to Trinity Trailhead.
routes.bail: The col at roughly 7,200 ft is a natural bail point with room to camp and water nearby; from there, descend back the way you came to the Chiwawa River Trail rather than continuing onto the ridge in deteriorating weather.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux is a short but genuinely exposed, unroped 4th-class step right at the summit, turn back before attempting it if the rock is wet or snow-covered, or if the party is moving slower than planned -- there is no easy retreat once committed to the final ridge pitch.
routes.bestSeason: June through October; crampons and an ice axe are commonly used on the snow slopes crossing to the Chiwawa-Fortress col even in late summer.
routes.itinerary: Typically a 2-3 day trip: hike in via the Chiwawa River/Buck Creek Trail to camp at the ~7,200 ft Chiwawa-Fortress col (or a 7,100 ft meadow plateau below Fortress), climb Fortress and often Chiwawa Mountain from camp, then hike back out. A car-to-car speed-traverse of both peaks (25+ miles, off-trail) has been done in just over 9 hours by a strong trail runner.
routes.detailedRack: No rack beyond a light alpine kit is typically carried -- ice axe and crampons for the snow crossing to the col, a helmet for the loose gully, and most parties climb the exposed 4th-class summit step unroped; a rope and a few slings can be worthwhile insurance for less experienced scramblers on that final step.
routes.proNeeds: Minimal fixed protection; this is unroped alpine scrambling with one genuinely exposed 4th-class move near the summit.
gear: Light rope + slings (optional, for the summit step)
whatToBring: Water treatment
Map/compass or GPS for cross-country basin travel
Extra socks for river fords
Layers for the exposed ridge
proTips: Rock quality improves noticeably once you gain the East Ridge crest itself -- the approach gully below it is the loosest section.
Carry crampons and an ice axe even in late summer; parties have needed firm snow skills for the traverse across to the col.
The route is often linked with Chiwawa Mountain via the shared 7,200 ft col, making a Chiwawa-Fortress traverse or double-summit day popular.
Trekking poles help on the scree/talus benches below the ridge.
watchOut: A short but genuinely exposed 4th-class step guards the final summit block -- check holds carefully.
The col-crossing snow slope steepens noticeably near the top and has required crampons/axe even in late-season conditions.
Route-finding through the upper Chiwawa Basin meadows and deadfall is nontrivial off-trail terrain.
knownHazards: Loose rock in the gully gaining the East Ridge from the Chiwawa-Fortress col.
Exposed, unroped 4th-class climbing on the final summit pitch.
Cold, thigh-to-knee-deep river fords crossing the Chiwawa River drainage on the approach.
Same FR 6200 road-closure access issue as the Southwest Face route (extra ~7 miles of road-walking each way since 2026).

routes.overview: A steeper, more technical alternative on Fortress's northeast-facing side: several pitches of rock up a headwall, followed by a long snow slope studded with rock islands. The route tops out on the ridge crest short of the true summit, requiring further ridge travel to finish.
routes.beta: Very little beta exists online for this line beyond a single Cascade Climbers forum report: it follows the left side of the visible northeast face, with steeper rock and snow options further right of uncertain quality/rock condition. Expect a mixed rock-and-snow outing rather than a simple scramble, with a rope needed for the pitched rock section. One climber who used it noted it is inaccurately described in Beckey's most recent Cascade Alpine Guide.
watchOut: Reported to be inaccurately described in Beckey's most recent guidebook -- expect route-finding uncertainty.
The route tops out on the ridge short of the true summit, requiring additional, largely unscouted ridge travel to reach the top.
knownHazards: Rockfall/loose-rock potential on the headwall pitches, with uncertain rock quality reported on right-hand variations.
An upper snow slope with rock islands that will vary significantly with seasonal snowpack.

blurb: West Craggy Peak (8,366 ft) is a subsidiary summit of Big Craggy Peak in the Okanogan Range of the North Cascades, rising above Eightmile Creek roughly 12 miles north-northeast of Mazama in Okanogan County, Washington. It straddles the Pasayten Wilderness boundary (terrain west of the crest lies within the wilderness) and is built of loose, fractured rock typical of the Okanogan Range's accreted-terrane geology, making it primarily a scree-and-talus scramble rather than a technical climb. West Craggy and neighboring Big Craggy Peak — together known as "The Craggies" — are usually climbed together via Copper Glance Basin and rank among the more accessible, trouble-free-access summits on Washington's "Bulger" list of the state's 100 highest peaks.

routes.routeName: Standard Route (South Ridge / South Gully via Copper Glance Basin)
routes.overview: The standard route climbs West Craggy's south side from a high camp in Copper Glance Basin: talus and scree lead to a gully/chute below the south ridge (often holding snow into summer), which tops out on an easy summit ridge walk. No roped climbing is required in summer conditions.
routes.beta: Approach via the Copper Glance Trail to Copper Glance Lake and the upper basin below Isabella Ridge, camp or day-trip from there, then ascend talus/scree and a south-facing gully to the south ridge and follow it to the summit. Commonly combined with an ascent of neighboring Big Craggy Peak (connected by a ridge/saddle) in the same trip. Loose rock (choss) and lingering snow in the gully are the main technical considerations; route is class 2 with brief class 2-3 scrambling in places.
routes.approach: From Winthrop, drive north about 8 miles on West Chewuch Road (County Road 1213) to Eightmile Road (Forest Road 5130), then continue roughly 12-15 miles northwest along Eightmile Creek to the Copper Glance Trailhead (about 3,800-3,810 ft; no fee). Hike Copper Glance Trail #519, which begins on an old mining-claim jeep track/tramway grade (FSR 505) for about 1.5 miles to the historic Copper Glance mine, crosses Copper Glance Creek (the creek's bridge has been reported washed out or missing in some years, requiring a ford), and continues through a 2018 burn area past a small pond to Copper Glance Lake and the open upper basin below Isabella Ridge (about 3.3-3.5 miles one-way, ~6,260-6,600 ft) — a common camp/base for the peak.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line: down the south ridge/gully to the basin and out the Copper Glance Trail.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the south ridge back into the gully/chute and descend the loose scree and talus to the upper basin. Where snow remains, an ice axe (and crampons/microspikes if firm) is useful, and glissading the snow sections can speed descent when conditions allow. Below the basin, retrace the Copper Glance Trail past the mine site and back down the old tramway grade to the trailhead, re-fording Copper Glance Creek where the bridge is out.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Drive to the Copper Glance Trailhead and hike ~3.3-3.5 miles with ~2,800 ft gain to a camp in Copper Glance Basin (~6,260-6,600 ft). Day 2: From camp, climb ~1,800 ft up talus/scree and the south gully to the south ridge and the summit (roughly 2.5-3 hours one-way), then descend to camp, break camp, and hike out. Strong parties often combine this with Big Craggy Peak and/or do the whole outing car-to-car in one long day.
routes.bail: Retreat is straightforward and non-technical: downclimb the same ascent gully/ridge back into Copper Glance Basin. There are no fixed rappel anchors on the route; any point on the south ridge or gully can be reversed with care on the loose rock.
routes.turnaround: Because the crux is a loose, rockfall-prone gully that is more hazardous to down-climb in soft afternoon snow or under other parties, set a turnaround time that gets you off the gully and summit ridge before afternoon thunderstorms typical of the East Cascades summer build, and before warming softens any remaining snow in the chute.
routes.bestSeason: Most commonly climbed June through September. Snow can linger in the summit gully into July, favoring an ice axe and sometimes crampons/microspikes for security; a documented winter ascent (probable first winter ascent, Feb 9, 2020) shows the route is feasible outside summer but adds cornices, avalanche-prone couloir terrain, and a much longer snowed-in road approach.
watchOut: Loose, chossy rock throughout the upper mountain — call out rockfall and avoid stacking below other parties in the gully
The bridge over Copper Glance Creek has been reported washed out or missing in some seasons, requiring a creek ford
Wind-exposed summit ridge with potential for fast-building afternoon thunderstorms
The 2018 burn area below camp adds deadfall/blowdown obstacles to the lower trail
knownHazards: Sustained loose talus and scree with real rockfall potential on the ascent/descent gully
Residual steep snow in the couloir/gully into early-mid summer, requiring an ice axe
Cornices and avalanche-prone terrain if attempted in winter or spring
High-water creek crossing at Copper Glance Creek in early season
routes.season: Jun-Sep (snow lingers into Jul in the summit gully)

routes.detailedRack: No technical rock rack is needed for the standard summer route. Carry an ice axe for the summit gully/chute whenever snow is present (common into July), with crampons or microspikes if the snow is firm; a helmet is recommended given the loose rock in the gully.

climate.typical: East-Cascades rain-shadow climate: warmer, drier summers than the west-side Cascades, with cold nights at elevation and potential for fast-building afternoon thunderstorms.
climate.spring: Heavy snowpack lingers into June on shaded aspects and in the summit gully; road access to the trailhead may still be snowbound.
climate.summer: Warm, mostly dry conditions July-September form the prime climbing window.
climate.fall: Clear, cool weather with larches turning gold in the surrounding basins by late September-early October; nights below freezing.
climate.winter: Cold with a snowed-in access road; a February 2020 trip recorded a probable first winter ascent under deep snow, wind, and cornice conditions.

blurb: Raven Ridge is an 8,572-foot summit in the Methow Mountains, a dry east-side sub-range of the North Cascades, rising above Libby Lake within the Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (Okanogan County). Built of loose granitic/metamorphic rock typical of the Methow high country, it is one of Washington's "Bulger" peaks (the state's 100 highest) and is most often combined with neighboring Hoodoo Peak via a connecting ridge traverse. No documented first-ascent record was found for the peak.

routes.routeName: Raven Ridge Standard Route (Libby Lake Gully-Ridge)
routes.rockGrade: Class 3–4 (scramble; one exposed no-fall Class 4 move near the true summit)
objHaz: Loose rock/rockfall in the ascent gully
Exposed Class 4 knife-edge sections on the summit ridge
Wind exposure on the open ridge crest
routes.season: Late May–October; early season (May–mid-June) still holds snow on the trail and gully and typically requires an ice axe and possibly crampons/snowshoes
routes.overview: The standard route follows the Libby Lake Trail to alpine Libby Lake, then climbs a steep, very loose ~900-ft talus gully to gain the Raven Ridge crest, finishing with an exposed Class 3–4 scramble east along the ridge to the true (east) summit at 8,572 ft.
routes.beta: From the Libby Lake Trailhead, hike the well-graded Libby Creek/Libby Lake Trail roughly 5.1–5.5 miles and about 3,185–3,210 ft of gain to Libby Lake (~7,618–7,640 ft). From the lake, locate the loose talus gully that accesses the east-west-oriented ridge (some parties report two gully options near the lake's south end; the standard line rises about 900 ft). The gully is loose and rockfall-prone — favor the climber's-left wall for firmer rock and to reduce hazard to parties below. Once on the ridge crest, an exposed Class 2–3 scramble of roughly half a mile leads toward the summit block, which has a short, unavoidable, no-fall Class 4 move (some reports mention a small tunnel feature en route). USGS topo maps mislabel the western "Libby" summit as the high point (8,580 ft); the true, higher summit is the eastern peak at 8,572 ft. A separate, lower sub-summit called "Corax" (~8,380 ft) sits just west of the main summit saddle. Strong parties often continue the ridge traverse north to Hoodoo Peak, which adds a long exposed Class 4 section and a 5.7 overhanging boulder problem best protected with a short rope.
routes.approach: From Twisp, drive WA-20 east a short distance to WA-153, then south/east on WA-153 (about 1.4–9.4 miles depending on the source's starting point) to Libby Creek Road (County Road 1049/1045). Follow Libby Creek Road to Forest Road 43 (turn, ~5.1–5.5 miles), then left onto Forest Road 4340 (~1.2–1.5 miles), right onto Forest Road 4340-700 (~1.5 miles), and left onto Forest Road 4340-750 to the Libby Lake Trailhead (4,400–4,430 ft; roadside parking for roughly 10 vehicles, no toilet or potable water). From the trailhead, follow Libby Creek Trail #415 to Libby Lake, watching for trail damage (root holes) from the 2018 Crescent Mountain Fire between roughly miles 0.8 and 2.5. A dilapidated cabin sits beside the trail at about 7,170 ft, used by some parties as a base camp.
routes.descent: Descend by reversing the ascent: downclimb the ridge scramble, then carefully downclimb the loose talus gully back to Libby Lake, and return via the Libby Lake Trail to the trailhead.
routes.descentText: There is no separate descent line for the standard route — parties reverse their ascent. The crux of the descent is the loose gully: move deliberately, stay to the climber's-left (looking downhill) on more solid rock where possible, and be conscious that dislodged rock can strike other parties below. No fixed rappel anchors exist on the standard line (a rappel station has been reported on the connecting ridge toward Libby Peak/Hoodoo Peak, not on the standard Raven Ridge summit route). Retrace the trail from Libby Lake back to the Libby Lake Trailhead.
routes.itinerary: Most parties climb Raven Ridge as a single long day from the Libby Lake Trailhead (roughly 10–11 miles round trip with about 4,100+ ft of gain). Parties combining Raven Ridge with Hoodoo Peak often backpack in about 5 miles to camp near the old cabin (~7,170 ft) or at Libby Lake, climb both peaks via the connecting ridge on day two, and hike out on day three (or as a very long single day for fit, fast parties).
routes.bail: Retreat is a straightforward reversal of the ascent line rather than a technical bail. The main challenge is downclimbing the loose gully carefully — stay on the more solid climber's-left rock and move one party member at a time to limit rockfall exposure to those below. There are no fixed rappel stations on the standard route to aid retreat.
routes.turnaround: Because a large share of the route is on loose, exposed rock with real rockfall and fall potential, begin early enough to be off the ridge and gully well before dark, and be willing to retreat if afternoon thunderstorms build — common on hot summer days on this dry, east-side range.
routes.bestSeason: Late May through October. Early-season ascents (May–mid-June) usually still have snow on the trail and in the ascent gully, and an ice axe (with crampons/snowshoes as conditions warrant) is recommended. Conditions are typically driest and the rock least icy from July through September. Late September into early October adds spectacular larch color along the approach, though early-season snow can arrive by then as well.
routes.detailedRack: No fixed protection or technical rack is needed for the standard scrambling line. A helmet is strongly recommended given the pervasive loose rock and rockfall risk in the ascent gully. Parties planning to continue the connecting ridge toward Hoodoo Peak (or wanting a safety net for the Class 4 summit move) sometimes carry a short 30–60 m rope or a sling, since a rappel station exists on that connector near a near-vertical step.
routes.proNeeds: None fixed on the standard route; an optional short rope/sling covers the exposed Class 4 summit move or the Hoodoo Peak ridge connector.
gear: 30–60 m rope (optional, for the Class 4 crux or Hoodoo connector)
whatToBring: Detailed map/GPS — route-finding through the talus gully and along the ridge is nontrivial
Sun protection (little shade on the open ridge)
Extra water (few reliable sources above Libby Lake late in season)
Insect repellent (mosquitoes reported near Libby Lake and the old cabin)
Wind layers for the exposed summit ridge
Satellite communicator (no cell coverage confirmed in the field reports reviewed)
proTips: Stay on the climber's-left (more solid) side of the ascent gully to reduce rockfall risk to yourself and anyone below.
USGS maps mislabel the western 'Libby' summit as the high point (8,580 ft); the true summit is the eastern peak at 8,572 ft — don't stop at the first high point reached.
Combine with neighboring Hoodoo Peak via the connecting ridge for a bigger day, but be ready for a long exposed Class 4 section and a 5.7 overhanging boulder problem on that link.
Visit in late September/early October for golden larch color along the Libby Lake Trail.
watchOut: Extremely loose talus and active rockfall throughout the ~900-ft ascent gully — helmet essential.
An exposed, no-fall Class 4 move guards the true (east) summit block.
Afternoon thunderstorms are common on hot East Cascades summer days.
Mosquitoes can be intense near Libby Lake and the old cabin in early-to-mid summer.
Trail sections between roughly miles 0.8 and 2.5 have burned-out root holes from the 2018 Crescent Mountain Fire.
knownHazards: Loose rock and rockfall in the ascent gully are the primary objective hazard and can injure parties climbing below.
A single unroped Class 4 move guards the true summit block, with real fall consequences.
The ridge crest is open and wind-exposed with little to no shelter.

pitchDetail.notes: ~5.1–5.5 mi one-way, ~3,185–3,210 ft gain, to Libby Lake (~7,618–7,640 ft); some fire-damaged tread between miles 0.8–2.5
~900 ft ascent gully; extremely loose talus — stay climber's-left for firmer rock and to limit rockfall onto others
~0.5 mi exposed scramble east along the ridge crest to the true (east) summit at 8,572 ft

road.name: Libby Creek Road (CR 1049/1045) to Forest Roads 43 → 4340 → 4340-700 → 4340-750
road.status: Paved county/state road (WA-20/WA-153, Libby Creek Road) transitioning to gravel USFS roads for the final approach to the trailhead
road.driveNote: From Twisp: WA-20 east to WA-153 south, then Libby Creek Road, FR 43, FR 4340, FR 4340-700, and FR 4340-750 to the Libby Lake Trailhead; roadside parking for roughly 10 vehicles.

climate.forecastZone: Not covered by NWAC's avalanche forecast zones (central/east-Cascades interior, incl. the Methow Valley, falls outside NWAC's zone coverage)
climate.typical: Dry, sunny East-Cascades continental climate — warm summers, cold snowy winters, and generally less precipitation than the west-side Cascades
climate.spring: Snow lingers on the trail and in the ascent gully into May–June; early starts and traction gear are common
climate.summer: Warm and mostly dry with real afternoon thunderstorm risk; mosquitoes can be heavy near Libby Lake and the old cabin
climate.fall: Prime season; larch turn gold in late September–early October, though early snow is possible by then
climate.winter: Heavy snow typically renders the approach road and trail impassable to most parties from roughly November through May

access.landManager: USDA Forest Service, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, Methow Valley Ranger District
access.fees: USFS's own Libby Lake Trailhead page and WTA state no parking fee or pass is required; note one source (Mountaineers.org) states a Northwest Forest Pass is required — confirm current signage locally
access.permit: No wilderness permit is required for day or overnight travel in the Lake Chelan-Sawtooth Wilderness
access.passRequired: Conflicting reports — USFS/WTA say none required at Libby Lake Trailhead; Mountaineers.org lists a Northwest Forest Pass
access.closures: No permanent closures reported; the trail carries fire damage (burned root holes) from the 2018 Crescent Mountain Fire between roughly miles 0.8 and 2.5; the route is generally snowbound and inaccessible from about November through May

emergency.rangerStation: Methow Valley Ranger District, 24 W Chewuch Rd, Winthrop, WA — (509) 996-4000
emergency.nearestHospital: Mid-Valley Hospital, 810 Jasmine St, Omak, WA — (509) 826-1760 (Three Rivers Hospital in Brewster, WA — (509) 689-2517 — is a comparable-distance alternate with a 24/7 ER)

routes.corrections: Given elevation (8,572 ft) and coordinates (48.237898, -120.331964) match SummitPost's documented true (east) summit almost exactly (48.23788, -120.33185) — no correction needed. However, sources conflict on peak naming/height: Wikipedia states the ridge's 'highest point is Corax Peak,' while SummitPost states the true, higher summit is an unnamed east peak (8,572 ft) and that 'Corax' is a distinct, lower sub-summit (~8,380 ft) west of the main summit saddle, and that USGS topo maps mislabel the western 'Libby' summit as higher (8,580 ft) when it is not the true high point. gainFt (4,142 ft) is a derived figure from sourced trailhead elevation (4,430 ft, USFS/WTA) and summit elevation (8,572 ft), not a single published composite statistic. distKm (8.2 km) reflects the sourced one-way trail distance to Libby Lake (WTA: 5.1 mi); SummitPost/Mountaineers report a slightly longer 5.5 mi to the lake, and roughly another 0.5 mi of gully/ridge scrambling remains beyond the lake to the true summit, which is not included in the distKm figure.

blurb: Flora Mountain (8,323 ft) is a remote summit in the North Cascades' "Stehekin Seven" peak group, rising in the Glacier Peak Wilderness south of the town of Stehekin, visible from the Lake Chelan ferry. Described by climbers as a "desolate pile of loose gneiss," it was first climbed on September 11, 1940 by Joe Leuthold and Eldon Metzger; today its non-technical southwest talus slope makes it a popular but logistically demanding objective for Washington's Top-100-peak baggers willing to commit to a multi-day, boat-access-only wilderness approach.

objHaz: no reliable water source on the upper mountain
biting insects at higher camps in summer
routes.overview: An unroped Class 2 talus and scree walk up Flora's southwest ridge/slope — the peak's easiest line and by far its most commonly climbed route, reached via a multi-day off-trail approach from the Devore Creek Trail out of Stehekin.
routes.beta: The standard route leaves the trail system at Bird Creek Camp (4,200 ft) on the Devore Creek Trail and goes cross-country: through open forest and meadow to a saddle just south of Enigma Peak (~6,400 ft), down a scree gully to cross upper Castle Creek, then up Flora's southwest ridge on talus and scree to the summit. It is rated the least difficult of the Stehekin-area 'Top 100' peaks (compare neighboring Devore, Tupshin, and Riddle) and requires no rope, rack, or technical rock climbing — just sturdy boots, off-trail navigation, and tolerance for loose rock. The peak's namesake first ascent (Leuthold/Metzger, 1940) is attested for the mountain overall, but no source confirms it used this specific southwest line, so it is not attributed to the route here. An alternate, longer approach reaches the same southwest slope/west cirque from Holden Village via Railroad Creek Road and Tenmile Pass, used mainly by parties combining Flora with Riddle Peak and Black Tower.
routes.approach: From Chelan, take the Lady of the Lake ferry (or a floatplane) to Stehekin Landing. Ride the NPS shuttle bus 4.4 miles up Stehekin Valley Road to Harlequin Bridge ($5 fare per trip reports), cross the bridge, and follow the road past Harlequin Camp and the Stehekin Airport to pick up the Stehekin River Trail. Follow this mostly flat trail southeastward about 3 miles to the signed Devore Creek Trail junction. The Devore Creek Trail then climbs steeply through rock bluffs and open forest for about 1 mile, enters the Glacier Peak Wilderness, and traverses brushy, dry hillside for roughly 3 more miles to Bird Creek Camp (4,200 ft) — the only designated camp in the area, with room for about three tents. From Bird Creek Camp, most parties do Flora as a long day round trip: bushwhack through open forest (spiderwebs, chest-high brush, and blowdown reported) toward the saddle south of Enigma Peak, then follow the route description above to the summit.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent line down the southwest talus/scree to the Enigma Peak saddle and back to Bird Creek Camp.
routes.descentText: Down-climb the southwest ridge/slope on the same loose talus and scree used for the ascent, back to the saddle south of Enigma Peak, then drop and re-cross upper Castle Creek before regaining the bushwhack corridor down to Bird Creek Camp. The loose, sometimes football-sized talus is reported as noticeably more tedious and unstable on descent than ascent — expect slower travel and watch for rock knocked loose onto climbers below.
routes.itinerary: Day 1: Ferry/floatplane from Chelan to Stehekin Landing, NPS shuttle to Harlequin Bridge, then hike the Stehekin River Trail (~3 mi) and Devore Creek Trail (~4 mi, steep then brushy) to Bird Creek Camp (4,200 ft). Day 2: Round-trip summit push from Bird Creek Camp via the saddle south of Enigma Peak, Castle Creek crossing, and southwest ridge talus to the 8,323-ft summit and back (roughly 6+ hours per trip reports). Day 3: Hike out to Stehekin and catch the return ferry/shuttle. Many parties extend the trip to combine Flora with Devore and/or Tupshin Peaks from the same camp.
routes.bail: There are no fixed anchors or established bail points on this scramble route. Retreat simply means reversing the talus ridge back to the saddle south of Enigma Peak and dropping back to the Castle Creek drainage and Bird Creek Camp along the same cross-country line used on the way up.
routes.turnaround: As with any long off-trail wilderness scramble, set a firm turnaround time that leaves enough daylight to re-cross the unmarked, brushy saddle and creek-crossing sections before dark, and build in margin for afternoon weather changes given the remote, boat-access-only egress.
routes.bestSeason: Trip reports favor mid-to-late summer through fall. An early-July ascent (July 2010) found lingering snow and a 'frigid summit,' so early season can still be wintry at this elevation. Multiple trip reports specifically recommend visiting around larch season (roughly late September) for more stable weather, drier talus, and better scenery; plan for the route to be most straightforward roughly August through October.
routes.detailedRack: None needed — the standard route is an unroped Class 2 talus/scree scramble with no technical rock, ice, or aid climbing.
routes.proNeeds: No rope or rack is used on the standard route. In early season, an ice axe may be worth carrying for lingering snow on the approach or upper slopes.
gear: map/compass or GPS for off-trail navigation
whatToBring: water treatment (no reliable water source on the upper mountain)
bear-resistant food storage for Glacier Peak Wilderness camping
insect repellent (heavy bug activity reported near 7,200 ft)
sun protection for exposed talus slopes
layers for a 'frigid summit' even in July per one trip report
proTips: Fall, roughly late September (larch season), is repeatedly cited in trip reports as the best combination of stable weather and scenery.
Flora is commonly combined with Devore Peak and/or Tupshin Peak from the same Bird Creek Camp or a higher 5,800 ft Bird Creek drainage camp, since all share the same remote approach.
Bird Creek Camp is the only designated tent camp on the Devore Creek Trail near Flora and is the standard base for a one-day round-trip summit push.
An alternate, longer approach from Holden Village via Tenmile Pass reaches the same southwest slope/west cirque and is used mainly by parties linking Flora with Riddle Peak.
watchOut: Loose, fist- to football-sized talus on the upper slopes shifts underfoot and is reported to be more unstable and tedious on the descent than the ascent.
The off-trail section above Bird Creek Camp involves dense brush, blowdown, and route-finding through a basin/saddle south of Enigma Peak before the talus begins.
No reliable water source is reported on the upper mountain above the Bird Creek/Castle Creek drainages.
Severe insect activity has been reported near 7,200 ft in summer.
knownHazards: The mountain's rock is described in multiple sources as a 'desolate pile of loose gneiss,' producing persistently loose footing on the summit talus.
This is a remote, multi-day wilderness approach reachable only via the Stehekin ferry/floatplane and shuttle, with no road access and long potential evacuation times if injured.
One trip report noted fine volcanic ash accumulation (about 2 inches) at higher elevations, adding to already loose footing.

pitchDetail.notes: About 3 miles, mostly flat, from the Harlequin Bridge shuttle stop to the signed Devore Creek Trail junction.

pitchDetail.pitch: Devore Creek Trail to Bird Creek Camp
pitchDetail.notes: About 4 miles: climbs steeply through rock bluffs and open forest for roughly 1 mile, enters Glacier Peak Wilderness, then traverses brushy, dry hillside about 3 more miles to Bird Creek Camp (4,200 ft).

pitchDetail.pitch: Bird Creek Camp to saddle south of Enigma Peak
pitchDetail.notes: Bushwhack through open forest and marshy/meadow basin (around 6,400 ft) toward a saddle between Enigma Peak and Point 7716.
Descend a class 2 scree gully to Castle Creek, cross through open forest, and gain the base of Flora's southwest ridge.
Talus and scree boulder-hop up the southwest ridge to the 8,323-ft summit; described as the least difficult of the Stehekin-area peaks climbed on this line.

road.name: Stehekin Valley Road
road.status: No public highway access; Stehekin is reached only by boat, floatplane, or trail. An NPS shuttle bus runs seasonally along Stehekin Valley Road.
road.driveNote: No road connects Stehekin to the outside highway network. Visitors take the Lady of the Lake ferry (or a floatplane) from Chelan to Stehekin Landing, then ride the NPS shuttle bus about 4.4 miles up Stehekin Valley Road to Harlequin Bridge ($5 fare per trip reports) to reach the Stehekin River/Devore Creek trailheads.

climate.typical: East-slope Cascades climate — drier and sunnier than the Cascade crest's west side, with warm summer days, cold nights, and snow persisting into early summer at higher elevations.
climate.summer: Prime climbing season overall, though an early-July ascent still encountered lingering snow and a 'frigid summit'; heavy insect activity has been reported near 7,200 ft.
climate.fall: Favored season in trip reports for more stable weather and larch color (roughly late September); nights turn cold and early-season snow becomes possible.

access.landManager: North Cascades National Park Complex / Lake Chelan National Recreation Area (NPS) for the Stehekin approach corridor; Glacier Peak Wilderness, Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest (USFS) for the Devore Creek Trail beyond the wilderness boundary, including Bird Creek Camp and the summit.
access.fees: No fee for the hike itself; the NPS shuttle bus from Stehekin Landing to Harlequin Bridge costs $5 one-way per trip reports, and the Lake Chelan ferry (Lady of the Lake) to Stehekin is a separate paid fare.
access.permit: No permit required for day hiking; camping at Bird Creek Camp falls within the Glacier Peak Wilderness (USFS), which does not require a wilderness permit, though the initial corridor out of Stehekin passes through NPS-managed Lake Chelan National Recreation Area.
access.passRequired: No parking pass required, per Washington Trails Association.

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning departure from Bird Creek Camp, allowing a full day for the round trip.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: 911, or Chelan County Sheriff's Office / Rivercom Dispatch at 509-663-9911 for search and rescue.
emergency.rangerStation: Golden West Visitor Center, Stehekin (North Cascades National Park Complex); general park contact 360-854-7200.
emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health (Lake Chelan Community Hospital), 110 S Apple Blossom Dr, Chelan, WA — accessible from Stehekin only by boat, floatplane, or helicopter.
emergency.notes: Stehekin has no road access to the outside world; search-and-rescue response and medical evacuation from the Flora Mountain area require boat, floatplane, or helicopter and can take substantially longer than a road-accessible trailhead.

routes.corrections: Elevation varies by source: Peakbagger and Wikipedia list 8,325 ft (2,537 m, older USGS/NAVD88-derived figure) and listsofjohn.com lists 8,328 ft, while trailcatjim.com reports the elevation as revised to 8,323 ft based on Eric Gilbertson's recent lidar analysis — consistent with this database's existing 8,323 ft figure, so no change is recommended, but the discrepancy is noted for transparency.
